RNA-FLEEK

Fast Lightweight Expression Exploration Kit — a browser-based single-cell RNA-seq visualizer with cell type annotation.

Navigation Sidebar & panels Embeddings Selection Cell types Slice Hidden collections View splits Export Annotation Lineage Gene expression Plots Pathways DEG analysis Data Data matrices & routing Sessions Undo & redo Settings Quick reference Methods Changelog
▼ Navigation

3D View uses an orbit camera. You rotate around a center point marked by the navigation cross (navcross) — a small axis cross that appears while you're actively interacting, then fades after you stop. Think of it as the anchor your camera orbits around: left-drag rotates the camera while the navcross stays fixed.

The navcross has a fixed world size, so zooming (scroll) makes it grow on screen — you're moving closer to a fixed point. Flying keeps it the same apparent size because you're moving the navcross itself. This distinction helps you see whether you're changing distance or translating through the cloud.

Moving the navcross: Pan (Ctrl/⌘+drag or right-drag) slides the navcross laterally. Fly (⌥ Opt+drag or middle-drag) pushes it forward/backward along the camera's line of sight. The camera follows in both cases.

The navcross is constrained inside a bounding sphere computed from the point cloud — you can fly through the cloud but the navcross can't leave it. When you push it against the boundary, a geodesic wireframe briefly flashes to show the constraint. It fades when you stop or pull back. Zoom-out is capped at 4× the cloud radius.

On first load, the camera performs a brief intro orbit — starting close and pulling back with a half-rotation — to convey that the space is 3D and interactive. Any click or scroll cancels it.

Double-click on any cell to fly the camera to that location and zoom in to the neighborhood. The fly-to animation smoothly centers the view and zooms to 35% of the cloud radius — the same distance every time, regardless of your starting zoom level. In 3D, the navcross moves to the target cell. This is useful for quickly inspecting a region of interest spotted in the overview.

Auto-rotate: An optional slow orbit can be toggled from the View panel. When active, a small "Auto" badge appears on the button. Any manual interaction pauses auto-rotation; it resumes when you stop. This is useful for presentations or when exploring the cloud's 3D structure passively.

2D View uses an orthographic camera with pan and zoom. Panning is limited so the cloud can't be dragged fully off-screen. Zoom-out shows about 2× the cloud extent; zoom-in goes to 500×. A crosshair appears at screen center during panning to aid orientation.

Zoom acceleration: scroll speed scales with flick velocity — a gentle scroll nudges, a fast flick covers significant distance. Both 2D and 3D share the same acceleration curve.

Modifier key hints — when you hold Shift, Option, or Ctrl, the hint bar at the bottom-left updates to show what action your next drag will perform (lasso, fly, pan, etc.) before you start moving.

Shortcut flash — when you press a keyboard shortcut, a brief overlay flashes on screen showing the action name and key combination. This helps learn shortcuts and confirms the action was received. Toggle it off in Settings if you prefer a quieter interface.

The 2D/3D toggle and Reset button are always accessible in the View panel header, even when the panel is collapsed.

Sidebar: press \ or click the « button to hide the sidebar and give the visualization full width. Press \ again or click » to restore it.

Panel layout shortcuts: press ⌥ Opt+\ to expand all sidebar panels at once (a snapshot of the current per-panel collapse pattern is saved). Press it again — when everything is already expanded — to restore the previous pattern. ⌃ Ctrl+⌥ Opt+\ is the symmetric counterpart that collapses all panels (or restores previous when already collapsed). The two share a snapshot, so you can bounce expand ↔ collapse and still recover your original layout with one keystroke. Manual click-toggle on any panel header invalidates the snapshot — once you start hand-editing the layout, "previous pattern" stops meaning anything coherent.

Action3D2D
RotateLeft-drag—
PanCtrl+drag or right-dragDrag
Fly through⌥ Opt+drag or middle-drag—
ZoomScroll wheelScroll wheel
ResetReset View button or R
Toggle 2D/3DD key
▼ Sidebar & panels

The sidebar is composed of independent panels (Data, View, Split Views, Selection Groups, Cell Types, Gene, Slice). Each panel has a header with a collapse chevron and a drag grip.

Collapse: click any panel's header (anywhere except the grip) to collapse or expand it. Collapsed state persists in the browser session.

Rearrange: each panel header shows a ⋮⋮ grip on the left. Click-and-drag the grip to reorder panels within the sidebar — a floating badge with the panel name follows the cursor, and a blue indicator bar appears above or below target panels showing the drop position. Release to drop.

Second column: drag a panel past the right edge of the sidebar and a second sidebar column appears (dashed "Drop panel here" placeholder). Drop a panel there and the column snaps in at a fixed width, shrinking the canvas to fit. Drag panels back to the left column to free up the space — the second column auto-hides when it becomes empty, and the canvas reclaims the width.

Layout persists in localStorage keyed by the browser, so the arrangement survives reloads and dataset changes.

Auto-fill: the Cell Types panel dynamically expands to fill any leftover vertical space in whichever column it lives in. Collapsing other panels gives it more room for the cluster list; expanding them takes it back.

Hide sidebar: press \ or click the « button to hide the sidebar entirely (both columns) for a full-width canvas. Press \ again or click » to restore it.

▼ Embeddings

RNA-FLEEK supports multiple dimensionality reduction methods, selectable from the Embedding dropdown in the View panel. Each offers a different trade-off between local structure (how well nearby cells in the original high-dimensional space remain nearby in the plot) and global structure (how well the overall distances and relationships between cell populations are preserved).

EmbeddingLocalGlobalNotes
UMAPExcellentPoorThe standard. Tight, well-separated clusters. But distances between clusters are unreliable — related cell types may be far apart, unrelated types may be neighbors. The embedding is non-deterministic (varies between runs).
PCAPoorExcellentThe most honest view. Distances are meaningful and proportional to true expression differences. Rare populations get crushed together, and you lose resolution on subtle subtypes — but what you see is real. No hyperparameters, deterministic, instant to compute.
PaCMAPVery goodGoodExplicitly optimizes both local and global structure using a three-stage weighted loss: far pairs first (get the global layout right), then near pairs (refine clusters). Related cell types stay closer together than in UMAP, while clusters remain well-separated. Deterministic given the same seed.

Practical guidance: Start with UMAP for exploration — the clusters are easiest to see and interact with. Switch to PCA to check whether two clusters that look separate in UMAP are genuinely distant in expression space, or whether UMAP is exaggerating a small difference. Use PaCMAP when you want the best of both worlds — it's the most balanced view for understanding how cell populations relate to each other.

Important UMAP caveat: UMAP's cluster sizes, shapes, and inter-cluster distances are all unreliable. A large cluster in UMAP may be smaller than a tiny one. Two adjacent clusters may be unrelated. Two distant clusters may be almost identical. UMAP only reliably tells you that cells within a cluster are similar — nothing about the relationships between clusters.

All embeddings are available in both 2D and 3D. UMAP and PCA are computed during initial data loading only if needed. PaCMAP computes in the background after loading — the dropdown shows "PaCMAP (computing…)" until it's ready. FLEEK-computed embeddings are cached to disk for instant reload on subsequent sessions.

Where embeddings come from. For each embedding, FLEEK uses this priority: (1) adata.obsm["X_umap"] / X_pca / X_pacmap if present and matching the cell count — always preferred, the file is the authoritative source. (2) A cached copy in .fleek_cache.npz — fallback when the file has no embedding of its own (usually the result of a previous FLEEK-computed session). (3) Compute from scratch via scanpy (UMAP) / PCA / a PaCMAP subprocess, using the matrix chosen by the Data matrices & routing logic (log-normalized preferred, raw counts + normalize+log1p as fallback). The Data panel's "Embeddings" line shows exactly where each one came from this session ("from obsm (author-provided)", "from cache (full)", "computed by FLEEK", etc.), so you never have to guess.

Input feature space (for FLEEK-computed embeddings). When FLEEK computes a UMAP or PaCMAP itself, it picks the highest-priority obsm rep available as input: X_scVI → X_scANVI → X_totalVI → X_pca, falling back to a freshly-computed PCA only when none of those exist. This matters because scVI-family files typically ship a 2D X_umap that was built on the scVI latent — picking that same latent for the 3D pass keeps both dimensions in the same coordinate system rather than forcing the 3D into PCA-of-X geometry. The HUD label in the canvas overlay shows the input alongside the embedding name (e.g. "UMAP · scVI" vs "UMAP · PCA"), and the Data panel's Embeddings section spells it out per dimension.

Native 2D embeddings: By default, FLEEK computes only the 3D embedding and derives 2D by slicing the first two coordinates — halving computation time. Enable Native 2D embeddings in the load options to compute 2D independently, which produces tighter cluster separation in 2D views at the cost of longer load times. If a native 2D cache exists from a previous load, it is automatically preferred regardless of the current setting.

Keyboard shortcuts: Press D to toggle between 2D and 3D views. Press E to cycle through available embeddings (UMAP → PCA → PaCMAP → …).

Recompute on demand. The Data panel has a collapsible Recompute subsection. Both UMAP and PaCMAP can be recomputed without restarting the server: the panel shows the current embedding source per dimension and exposes the parameters that matter. UMAP knobs are QuickMap (subsample-and-project on / off), Fit cells (subsample size), n_neighbors, and min_dist; PaCMAP knobs are the analogous QuickPaCMAP, Fit cells, n_neighbors, and Project k (k-NN size for the projection step on subsampled cells). Recompute overwrites the matching cache variant on disk (only the variant that matches your current QuickMap setting — the other variant is left intact, so you can flip back without recomputing). PCA is read-only; FLEEK uses adata.obsm['X_pca'] when present or computes it once at load. The new coordinates appear in the viewer as soon as the recompute returns; a confirm dialog shows the chosen settings before the work starts so you don't overwrite cache by accident.

▼ Selection groups

Selection groups let you define populations of cells for comparison (e.g. DEG analysis). Cells belong to at most one group. Groups are color-coded with a number badge showing the keyboard shortcut.

ActionHow
Lasso selectShift+drag on UMAP
Lasso deselectShift+⌥ Opt+drag
Single cellShift+click on UMAP
Entire cell typeShift+double-click on UMAP, or
Shift+click in Cell Types panel
Activate/create groupClick group row, or Shift+1–9 (creates if needed)
Toggle visibilityClick the color dot, or ⌥ Opt+Shift+1–9
Hide/restore allH (press again to restore previously visible)
Solo group⌥ Opt+click the dot (click again to restore)
Select all visible cellsShift+V
Clear visible cellsEsc
Clear ALL cells (all groups)⌥ Opt+Esc
RenameDouble-click the name
Export subsetClick the download icon (downloads .h5ad)

Cells are assigned to the active group (highlighted row). Groups are non-overlapping: adding a cell to one group removes it from any other.

Highlight modes (Settings): Ring (default) draws a colored ring around selected cells, preserving the underlying cluster/expression color — ideal for seeing how selected cells relate to expression patterns. Fill replaces the cell color entirely with the group color. In both modes, non-selected cells can be faded using the Unselected Brightness slider in the View panel — slide left to dim unselected cells, right for full brightness. Selected cells are never dimmed.

Brightness auto-adjust: When you create your first selection, the slider automatically moves to a comfortable dim level so your selection stands out. You can adjust it freely from there. When you clear all selections (Escape), the slider resets to full brightness. When all highlighted cells are hidden — whether by hiding groups, hiding clusters, or slicing — brightness auto-resets to full.

Picking at zoom-out: In 3D, the raycaster's world-space threshold naturally makes distant points easier to hit. In 2D, each point's pixel pick radius has a floor of ~8 CSS pixels, so individual cells remain clickable even when zoomed far out and the dots render as 1–2 px dots. When zoomed in close, the floor is a no-op — the usual per-point radius applies, so precision isn't lost.

▼ Cell types

The Cell Types panel lists all cluster annotations from the h5ad file with per-cluster cell counts. It offers two views, toggled by the List / Lineage segmented button:

List view (default) — a flat, scrollable list of all cell types. Each row shows a color dot, cell type name, annotation predictions (if computed), and cell count. Empty clusters (0 cells) appear dimmed and non-interactive — these occur when a dataset was subsetted without cleaning categorical columns.

ActionHow
Show/hide typeClick the row (dot fills = visible, ring = hidden)
Solo type⌥ Opt+click (click again to restore)
Select into groupShift+click (toggles this type into / out of the active group)
Isolate in group⌥ Opt+Shift+click (clears the active group, then selects only this type)
Show/hide allall / none buttons in header
SortA→Z (alphabetical) / #↓ (by count)
SearchType in the search box — filters by name and annotations

Lineage view — a developmental hierarchy tree. See the Developmental Lineage section below for details.

Hidden cell types are removed from the 3D/2D view entirely (exiled off-screen), not just faded. Hovering over a cell in the UMAP shows the cluster name, gene expression (if active), group membership, and annotation predictions (if computed). The tooltip in lineage view also shows the original cluster name from the file if it differs from the lineage node name.

Annotation buttons: Markers and Claude run cell type inference (see Annotation section). After completion, buttons show a ✓ and become inactive. Clearing their cache from the Data panel re-enables them.

Search: The search box filters by cluster name and annotation predictions in both views. In lineage mode, matching branches auto-expand and non-matching branches hide. Typing "monocyte" will show clusters whose name or annotation contains "monocyte."

Auto-expand to fill column: The Cell Types panel dynamically grows to fill leftover vertical space in whichever sidebar column it lives in. Collapsing any other panel gives the cluster list more room; expanding one takes it back. When Cell Types sits above other panels (due to drag-reordering), it only grows down to the next panel's top edge — it won't overlap what's below.

▼ Slice panel

The Slice panel filters cells by obs metadata columns (organ, timepoint, batch, etc.). Each column appears as a row of pill toggles — one per unique value in that column.

ActionHow
Toggle a valueClick its pill
Solo a value⌥ Opt+click (hides all others in that row)

Empty values (cells with no entry for a column) are hidden by default. Click the N empty toggle (per column) or the global Show Empty toggle in the footer to reveal them — the label highlights when empty values are visible. Counts next to each column header show non-empty active / total values.

Propagation: the global Show Empty toggle isn't just cosmetic — empty values are hidden everywhere they'd be enumerated in the app. With Show Empty off, the Plots-panel Facet dropdown counts only non-empty values toward its "≥ 2 values" threshold (so a mostly-empty column drops out of the picker), and the DEG pseudo-bulk Replicate column dropdown uses the same non-empty count for its 2–50 eligibility. Flip the toggle on to bring empties back into every list in one go. Empty cell types (clusters with 0 cells) are unconditionally hidden from plots and DEG pickers regardless of this toggle.

Cells that fall outside the current filter are exiled from the visualization — they are not just faded, they are removed entirely. All downstream tools (selection, DEG analysis, cell type visibility) respect the active slice.

Download slice: Click the download button to export all cells matching the current filter as an .h5ad file.

Session auto-save captures the current slice state, so your filter persists across browser sessions.

▼ Hidden collections & embedding picker

The Info HUD overlay (bottom-left of the canvas, one per split when split mode is on) has two clickable rows that surface inline drop-up menus:

  • Hidden N — click to open the Unhide: menu. Lists every hidden cell type (with its colored dot), hidden slice values grouped by obs column, hidden selection groups, and arbitrarily-hidden cells (via Delete/Backspace). The first item is all — one click restores everything in that split. Each individual row brings that one back.
  • Embedding — click to open the embedding switcher. Lists every loaded embedding (UMAP, PCA, PaCMAP) with its provenance suffix; click one to switch this split's embedding. Tethered halves switch together (since they share a camera and so must share coords); untethered halves switch independently.

Either popup closes on outside click or Esc. The Hidden row dims (no caret, no hover highlight) when nothing's hidden in that split — there's nothing to unhide.

Per-split scope. Cell-type, slice, and arbitrary visibility live on each split's snapshot, so each split's Hidden popup reflects only what's hidden in THAT split. Selection-group visibility is global across splits (so cross-split DEG works on a shared set), so unhiding a group from any split's popup affects every split.

Undo. Every unhide and every embedding switch pushes to the history stack, so Cmd/Ctrl+Z (or the Back arrow in the top-right) unwinds them step-by-step.

▼ View splits

View splits are named snapshots of your current slice filter and cluster visibility. They let you save and switch between different "angles" on the data — for example, one split filtered to organ=heart, another to organ=liver — without losing state.

Create a new view split with the + button in the Split Views panel, or press 1–9 to create/switch to "Split N" by number. You can create as many splits as you want. Switch between them using the sidebar dropdown, or press the number key matching the split name. Each split remembers its slice filter, cluster visibility, deleted cells, color mode + active gene, and camera position — switching between splits preserves where you were looking in each one, even outside of split-screen mode.

Selections are global across splits. Selection groups, group visibility, and the active group are not part of a split's state — they stay the same when you switch splits. This lets you build a selection in one split and use it in another (run DEG across splits, carry a lasso of interesting cells through different slice filters, etc.). The same shared-selection behavior already applies inside split-screen mode.

Split-screen (⧉): When you have two or more view splits, click the split-screen button to show two side by side in real time. Each half renders its own cell population based on its split's slice filter. Selection groups are shared across both halves — lasso in one half, see the highlights in both. A subtle vertical divider marks the boundary between the halves; the accent-colored focus border on the active half overlaps it on that side.

Reassigning halves: The split name in each half's label is a dropdown. Change it to show a different split in that half — if you pick a split already in the other half, they swap. Each half's camera state follows the split shown there.

FeatureDetails
Camera tetheringBy default cameras are tethered (they move together). Click the chain-link icon on a half's label, or on any view row in the Split Views panel, to untether — that view keeps its own independent camera. Tether state is persisted per view and works in single-view mode too: switching between two tethered splits keeps the same camera position, while switching to or from an untethered split restores that split's own saved camera.
Per-split embeddingTethered halves share an embedding (since they share a camera, they must share coordinate space) — changing the embedding from the View panel, E, or any tethered half's per-half dropdown switches every tethered half together. Untethered halves manage their own embedding independently — changing one of them touches only that half. The per-half dropdown is shown whenever ≥2 embeddings are loaded. Re-tethering a split snaps its embedding to the tethered group's so cameras and coords stay consistent; untethering leaves the embedding alone so you can immediately diverge.
Navcross behaviorIn 3D, the orbit-pivot cross stays at the active view's target, so it's always centered in the active half and drifts in the inactive half as you pan. 2D uses a fixed-size screen crosshair in the active half only.
FocusClick a split in the sidebar dropdown or use the number keys to focus a half. The focused half gets a highlight border.
NavigationMouse interactions (rotate, zoom, pan, lasso) affect the half the cursor is over. Dragging across the divider stays locked to the original half. Fly/pan direction is computed from the hovered view's own orientation, so untethered halves navigate correctly regardless of where the other view is pointing.
Double-clickZoom-focus works in each half independently (untethered only).
Per-split coloringEach half has its own color mode and active gene. One split can show gene X, the other can show gene Y, or one clusters and the other gene expression. Switch the active split to see its coloring reflected in the sidebar (gene search, color dropdown, expression legend).
Gene indicator pillA small pill at the top of each view shows the gene currently coloring it — top-left in single-view mode, top-center of each half in split-screen. Hides automatically when the view is in cluster-color mode.
Download iconEvery view row (and every split label at the top of the canvas) has a download icon. Click it to open the Export modal for that split's currently-shown points — see the Export section below.
Expression glowGlow is per-split — it only appears on halves currently in gene-expression mode. Toggling the Glow switch refreshes all halves.
HUDEach half has its own info overlay in the bottom corner: visible / hidden / total cells, the active Color mode (cluster, gene · NAME, or gene coloc · A + B), and the active selection group when one is selected. The Color row is filled per-half from each split's own snapshot, so it always describes the dots in the half it's under (not the active half's globals).
UndoEntering and exiting split-screen is captured in undo history. All changes within split-screen are undoable.
▼ Export

Every view split has its own Export action, triggered by the download icon on each row in the Split Views panel or on the view label at the top of the canvas. Exports what's currently shown in that split — cells that pass the split's slice filter, cluster visibility, and deleted-cell list, colored exactly as you see them on screen. Cells you've hidden from the main view (via the Cell Types panel, Slice panel, or Delete key) are excluded from the export. Ghost (zero-expression) cells in gene mode follow the main view's "Show non-expressing" toggle by default — if you can see them, they're exported. SVG additionally lets you override that toggle inline.

The Export modal offers three formats:

FormatWhat it gives youOpens in
SVGVector render of the split's current camera view. Each cell becomes a <circle> with its rendered color, opacity, and per-point pixel size (matching the shader's size-attenuation formula). Selection-group highlight rings optionally included as outline circles. Both 2D and 3D views supported — 3D uses back-to-front depth sorting so layering matches the on-screen image.Illustrator, Inkscape, Figma, browser
PLYTrue 3D point cloud with x/y/z + RGBA per vertex. Binary (compact) or ASCII (human-readable). 3D view only — disabled in the modal when you're in 2D since a flat PLY isn't useful.Blender, MeshLab, CloudCompare, ParaView
CSVTabular columns: coordinates, cluster ID + name, RGB + alpha, gene expression (when in gene mode), rendered pixel size, selection-group ID. Each column is opt-in.Anything tabular — R/ggplot, Python/matplotlib, Excel

Active camera: SVG uses the camera currently in use for that split — what's rendered is what gets exported. If you're in split-screen mode, each half uses its own camera. If the split isn't currently rendered (not in a split half, not the active single-view), SVG falls back to the split's saved camera snapshot.

File naming: every download in FLEEK follows the same template — <dataset-stem>_<what>_<detail>_<YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS>.<ext> — so files are self-describing in a downloads folder and reruns never silently overwrite. Examples: split SVG/PLY/CSV uses <stem>_<split-name>_<gene-or-pathway>_<ts>.<ext>; plot SVGs use <stem>_raincloud_FOXP3_<ts>.svg / <stem>_dotplot_FOXP3-CD4-IL2_<ts>.svg (faceted plots add _by-<facet>); volcano PNG uses <stem>_volcano_singlecell_GroupA_vs_GroupB_<ts>.png; DEG TSV uses <stem>_DEG_pseudobulk_<A>_vs_<B>_<ts>.tsv; subset h5ads use <stem>_subset_<group>_<N>cells_<ts>.h5ad. The modal shows an estimated file size before you commit.

Selection-group subset export: Each selection group in the Selection Groups panel has its own download icon that exports just the cells in that group as an .h5ad subset (the server writes a proper AnnData file). For exporting all cells matching a slice filter (not a selection), use the download button in the Slice panel header.

DEG results: The DEG panel has its own download icon that writes the current DEG table as TSV.

▼ Cell type annotation

RNA-FLEEK can predict cell type identities for each cluster using two complementary approaches. Both are accessed via buttons in the Cell Types panel header.

Markers (green M tag) — scores each cluster's differentially expressed genes against curated marker gene databases. For each cluster, a one-vs-rest Wilcoxon test produces a ranked list of upregulated genes (top 200 by default). Housekeeping / mitochondrial / ribosomal genes (MT-*, RPL*, RPS*, ACTB, GAPDH, B2M, …) are stripped before scoring — they dominate top-DEG lists in nearly every cluster and otherwise produce spurious matches with cell types that happen to list them as markers. The remaining genes are compared against known cell type signatures using two metrics:

MetricWhat it measures
Rank-weighted score (0–1)Each marker found at rank r in the cluster's DEG list contributes 1/log2(r+2) — so a marker at the top counts much more than one at rank 200. The sum is normalized by the best-case (all signature markers stacked at the top), giving a value in [0,1]. Rewards specifically- and strongly-upregulated markers, not just presence in a long list.
Fisher's exact testStatistical significance of the overlap, with the universe restricted to genes actually expressed in this dataset (≈ 5–15k) rather than the full ~30k gene-symbol space. Predictions are ranked by p-value first, then by weighted score.

The built-in database covers ~55 common human cell types. For broader coverage, run python scripts/download_markers.py (CellMarker2, ~800 types) and optionally python scripts/download_panglao.py (PanglaoDB, ~250 types). When PanglaoDB is present, the annotator ensembles both databases: a cell type that both DBs flag for the same cluster gets a "both" tag and the better-of-two p-value, which is a stronger signal than either DB alone.

Why marker-based annotation works: Cell types are defined by characteristic gene expression programs. A cluster whose top DEGs include CD3D, CD8A, GZMK is almost certainly a CD8+ T cell because those genes are specifically and consistently expressed in CD8+ T cells across many studies. The rank-weighted score quantifies how strongly the cluster's most distinguishing genes match a signature.

Limitations: Marker databases are incomplete and biased toward well-studied cell types. Novel or transitional states may not match any known signature. Small clusters (<5 cells) are skipped because they lack statistical power for DEG.

Claude (purple AI tag) — sends each cluster's top marker genes to Claude (Anthropic's LLM) for interpretation. This provides a different perspective:

AdvantageWhy
Broader knowledgeClaude has internalized marker gene associations from the full biomedical literature, not just a curated list. It can recognize rare cell types, transitional states, and tissue-specific subtypes.
ReasoningEach prediction includes a brief explanation citing specific markers, helping you evaluate the call. Hover over the AI tag to see the reasoning.
Context-awareClaude considers the gene combination holistically — it knows that CD14+LYZ+S100A8 together specifically indicate monocytes, not just that each gene is individually associated with monocytes.

Setup: Set the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable, or add it to ~/.fleek.env (recommended for shared servers). Claude annotation requires API credits (a few cents per dataset).

How it works: The server runs one-vs-rest DEG, sends the top 25 marker genes for each cluster to Claude in batches of 50 clusters. Claude returns a cell type prediction, confidence level (high/medium/low), and reasoning for each. Results are displayed as purple AI tags under each cluster name.

Using both together: The two approaches are complementary and share their most expensive computation — the one-vs-rest DEG analysis runs once and is reused by both. If you click Markers and Claude simultaneously, the DEG runs once and both methods proceed with their unique scoring steps in parallel. Marker DB annotation is instant after DEG; Claude requires API calls but shows live progress on the button ("Claude API batch 3/14… (35s)").

QuickDEG: For large datasets (100k+ cells), annotation DEG can take many minutes. The QuickDEG option (on by default) subsamples cells before running DEG — stratified by cluster to preserve proportions. With 50k cells, marker genes are virtually identical to the full dataset but computation is 10–20× faster. Quick and full DEG results are cached separately in memory — toggling QuickDEG on/off doesn't discard the other version. After both have been computed once, switching between them is instant.

Caching: Both marker and Claude annotations are cached to disk alongside the h5ad file (.annot_markers.json / .annot_llm.json). On subsequent loads, annotations appear instantly without recomputation or API calls. The shared DEG results are also cached in memory for the duration of the session. After annotation completes, the button shows a ✓ and becomes inactive — to re-run, delete the cache from the Data panel badges (hover over the badge, click ×).

▼ Developmental lineage

The Lineage button (yellow) in the Cell Types panel constructs a developmental hierarchy of the cell types in your dataset using Claude AI. Instead of a flat list, cell types are organized into a tree showing their developmental relationships — from common progenitors down to differentiated types.

How it works: The server sends all cluster names (plus Claude cell type predictions if available) to Claude with a prompt asking it to construct a developmental lineage tree. Claude uses its knowledge of developmental biology to infer the hierarchy, including intermediate progenitor stages that may not be present in the dataset.

Node styleMeaning
Normal textCell type present in the dataset (maps to one or more clusters)
Italic, fadedInferred intermediate — a developmental stage needed to connect the lineage but not represented in the dataset

Interactions:

ActionEffect
Click node nameToggle visibility of sub-lineage (same as dot)
Click caret (▸/▾)Collapse/expand that branch
Click color dotToggle visibility of entire sub-lineage on UMAP
Shift+clickToggle entire sub-lineage in/out of active selection group
⌥ Opt+Shift+clickToggle only this node's cells (not descendants)
⌥ Opt+clickSolo this sub-lineage (hide all other cell types)

Dots use the same filled/empty convention as the flat list — filled = visible, ring = hidden. Colors match the UMAP point colors and flat list. Hover over a node to see the original cluster name from the file, cell count, and interaction hints.

Caveats:

The lineage tree is AI-generated and should be treated as an informed hypothesis, not ground truth. Key limitations:

ConsistencyClaude may produce slightly different trees for the same cell types across runs. Results are cached after the first call, so the tree is stable within a session and across reloads.
Tree simplificationReal developmental biology involves convergence, transdifferentiation, and context-dependent paths. The tree format forces a simplified view — some cell types that arise from multiple progenitors are placed in one branch.
Tissue contextThe tree is most accurate when Claude annotations have already been run, providing tissue context. Without them, the hierarchy is inferred solely from cluster names.
NamingInferred intermediate nodes use standard developmental nomenclature, which may not match dataset-specific naming conventions.

Caching: The lineage tree is cached to .annot_lineage.json. Subsequent loads skip the API call. To regenerate, delete the Lineage cache badge from the Data panel (hover, click ×) and click Lineage again.

Best practice: Clicking Lineage automatically runs Claude annotation first if it hasn't been done yet — Claude-inferred cell types are far more reliable for lineage construction than raw cluster names, which may be generic (e.g. "Cluster 0"), abbreviated, or domain-specific. If Claude annotations already exist, the lineage builds directly from them. The lineage prompt instructs Claude to use the predicted cell type names for tree nodes.

Search: The type search box works in lineage mode too — matching nodes are shown with their parent branches auto-expanded, non-matching branches are hidden. This lets you find specific cell types within the hierarchy.

▼ Gene expression

The Gene panel is always visible in the sidebar. Switch to Gene Expression coloring with G (single gene) or L (coLocalization — two-gene merge). Press C to switch back to cell type coloring. Type a gene name in the search box — results appear in a scrollable dropdown. Click a row to load that gene, or use ↓ / ↑ to walk the list and Enter to pick the highlighted row (pressing Enter with nothing highlighted picks the first match). When the panel is collapsed, the currently selected gene name appears in the header.

GO term filtering — toggle the GO button next to the search box to switch the input into Gene Ontology mode. The same dropdown style applies: type ≥2 characters to see matching GO terms grouped by namespace (BP / MF / CC). Hover any row for the full domain name (Biological Process, Molecular Function, or Cellular Component) plus the dataset gene-count for that term. Use ↓ / ↑ + Enter to add a term, the same as for genes. Multiple terms can be combined as ANY (union) or ALL (intersection); the active filter is shown as removable chips below the search box.

Single Expression vs Colocalization. At the top of the Gene panel is a two-button toggle:

  • Single — the default. One gene, colored on the active gradient, with the full expression legend and gradient picker described below.
  • Colocalization — pick two genes to overlay as independent color channels. Cells bright in both light up in a composite "coloc" color (e.g. near-white on dark, near-black on light); cells bright in only one show that channel's color. The panel shows two channel slots — click a slot to mark it the active channel, then pick a gene from the list / search / dropdown / table below. Every pick (click in a row, j/k navigation, arrow + Enter in the search dropdown) routes into the active slot and leaves the active slot put, so you can scroll through the gene table comparing candidates without the highlighted slot ping-ponging. To fill the second slot, click the other slot row first, then pick a gene. Each slot has an × to clear it and a color dot that reflects the current channel palette.

Colocalization colorblind-safety: the default palette is magenta + green, which is discriminable for all forms of color-vision deficiency (red-green deficiency is by far the most common; magenta and green remain distinct there). Uncheck the Colorblind-safe toggle to switch to red + green if you specifically want to match a figure that uses the classic pair. Each channel's expression is normalized independently (per-gene 98th-percentile of expressing cells), so intensity is comparable across channels regardless of absolute expression levels. Coloc state (mode + both gene names + colorblind toggle) is persisted to sessionStorage so your setup survives page reloads.

Coloc legend bar. The horizontal strip under the slot row is a 3-stop ramp that reads left-to-right as R-only → both (composite coloc color) → G-only. It's the palette of cell colors you can see on the canvas — not a per-cell value scale, just "what colors mean what here." Earlier versions also faded the bar's edges to the canvas background to suggest "non-expressing cells", but the canvas already shows that, and on a dark sidebar the fades read as accidental black borders — so the ends are now solid R / G respectively.

Cluster color luminance clamp. FLEEK sources cell-type colors from (in order) adata.uns["fleek_color_map"], adata.uns["{cluster_col}_colors"], then a 24-color built-in palette. Some saved palettes contain very-dark or very-light entries that disappear against same-tone theme backgrounds. The client clamps every color's Rec. 601 luminance into [0.18, 0.85] at read time: too-dark colors get blended toward white, too-light toward black. The hue is preserved as much as possible, just nudged out of the invisible zones. This is automatic — no setting to flip — and applies to every callsite (canvas, legend, tooltips, exports) since the clamp lives inside the shared cc() color resolver.

Coloc Display units — the coloc tab offers a restricted set of display units: Normalized [0, 1], log1p(CP10K), and log1p(CPM). The full Single-tab list (which also includes Raw counts and adata.X (as-is)) is intentionally NOT offered here. The reason: coloc mixes two channels into one composite color, and the mixing only reads correctly when both channels are on comparable scales. Raw-count distributions across two genes can differ by orders of magnitude (a housekeeping gene maxing at hundreds vs a marker gene maxing at single digits), so a raw-count-driven mix would visually drown out the lower-count gene even when its relative expression is high. The three offered units are all monotonic transforms of each other after per-channel p98-normalization, so they produce essentially identical coloring — the picker mostly chooses the unit that reads in the cell tooltip. Picking one of the three writes to the same per-split geneDisplayUnit the Single tab uses; switching tabs preserves the choice when it's compatible.

Coloc dot size convention — when expression-driven sizing is on, a coloc cell's dot area is proportional to the sum of its two channel intensities (each channel in [0, 1] after per-channel p98-normalization). So a cell with R = G = 1 gets twice the area of a cell expressing only one channel at 1.0; a cell with R = G = 0.5 gets the same area as one expressing a single channel at 1.0. The sqrt of the sum drives the radius, so cells co-expressing both genes pop visibly without dominating the canvas; cells expressing only one channel stay in their normal single-gene size band. The same convention holds regardless of which Display unit is picked — the underlying [0, 1] per-channel normalization is what feeds the size encoding, so dot magnitudes don't shift when you switch between Normalized and log1p views.

Interaction with the gradient picker: single-gene mode uses the selected gradient (Viridis, Plasma, etc.). Colocalization mode ignores the gradient entirely — the two channel colors are fixed by the colorblind-safe toggle. Raincloud plots always use cluster colors, so colocalization mode doesn't affect them.

Expression values are fetched on demand from the server and cached client-side. Values are normalized 0–1 using the 98th percentile of expressing cells, then mapped to a color gradient. The legend appears inside the Gene panel whenever a gene is selected.

Cell tooltip — raw counts and library size. When you hover a cell in single-gene mode, the tooltip shows the gene's normalized expression (e.g. Olfm4: 38.4%) and, when raw counts are available, appends (N raw / lib M): N is the unnormalized integer count for that gene in that cell, and M is the cell's library size (sum of all raw counts across genes for that cell). The two together make the percentage interpretable: normalization divides counts by per-cell library size, so a cell with fewer raw counts but a smaller library will read higher as a percentage than a cell with more raw counts and a larger library — that's exactly what library-size normalization is for, and seeing both numbers in the tooltip makes the relationship explicit. The library size is fetched once per dataset and reused across all gene tooltips.

Gradient picker: click the gradient bar (or the "Viridis ▾" button next to the "Expression" label) to choose a different colormap. The dropdown lists every available gradient grouped by character, each with a live preview strip and a one-line note:

  • Auto (default) — resolves to Viridis in dark mode and Blues in light mode. Matches FLEEK's historical look and usually reads well on either background without you having to pick a colormap per theme.
  • Perceptually uniform — Viridis, Inferno, Plasma, Cividis, Rocket, Mako, Batlow. These map equal steps in the data to equal perceived lightness differences, so "twice as bright" really means twice as much expression. Cividis and Viridis are fully colorblind-safe; Batlow (Crameri) is well-tested for scientific publication.
  • High contrast / specialty — Turbo (Google's improved rainbow — wide dynamic range, but the hue jumps between blue/green/yellow/red can mislead about magnitude), Lajolla (Crameri warm sequential).
  • Single-hue — Blues, Reds, YlOrRd (ColorBrewer). Classic heatmap look; clean on a light background; trivially mappable to "low → high" in print. Reds is intuitive for "high expression = red" when reading alongside biologists used to Seurat's default.

The choice is persisted across sessions via sessionStorage. It applies to the main point cloud, the legend bar, and every color-baked export (SVG / PLY / CSV) so screen and file match exactly. Raincloud plots continue to use cell-type colors (not gene expression), so the gradient choice doesn't affect them.

When Gene Expression coloring is active, additional controls appear in the View panel:

  • Non-expressing cells — toggle visibility of cells with zero detected expression. When on (default), they appear as subtle ghost dots; when off, they are hidden entirely.
  • Expression → size — scales point area proportionally to expression level. High-expressing cells appear larger; non-expressing cells shrink to tiny dots.
  • Expression glow — adds a halo glow around expressing cells, making them stand out at a distance. The glow size adapts with zoom level.
  • Pulse expressing cells — slowly pulses expressing cells and their glow brighter (towards white in dark mode, deep navy in light mode) to help spot them. The halo also breathes in size with the pulse.

A hidden cell counter appears in the bottom-left corner of the canvas when any cells are invisible (hidden clusters, slice filter, or non-expressing toggle).

Gene Variability — a ranked list of all genes shown below the search box. The label "Ranking of gene variability in:" precedes a dropdown that switches between two modes:

  • Cell Type / Cluster (default) — shows per-cluster gene rankings from the shared one-vs-rest DEG analysis (the same computation used by Claude annotation). A dropdown selects the cell type (showing Claude-inferred names when available). A colored dot to the left of the dropdown reflects that cluster's visibility — click it to toggle, Alt+click to solo, Shift+click to select into a group. The column header shows the current sort metric and clicks to cycle through: |Cohen's d| → log₂FC → −log₁₀(padj). Bars are colored by direction: accent for upregulated genes, red for downregulated. Hovering over any gene shows all three metrics in the tooltip. The DEG computation triggers automatically on first use and is cached for the session.
  • All Cells (Global) — genes ranked by variability score, measuring how much more (or less) variable each gene is than expected for its expression level. When a dataset already contains a variability metric (e.g. dispersions_norm from scanpy), that metric is used directly. When no pre-computed metric exists, FLEEK computes its own using the locally standardized trend residual method — see Methods & Statistics below for full details. HVG genes appear bold with accent-colored bars.

When to use each metric:

  • |Cohen's d| (default) — standardized effect size: the mean difference divided by the pooled standard deviation. Tells you how cleanly separated a gene's expression is between this cell type and all others. Robust to sample size — a gene with a large d is a reliable marker regardless of how many cells you have. Best for finding definitive marker genes that distinguish a cell type.
  • log₂FC — log fold change: how many doublings of expression this cell type has over the rest. Directly interpretable in biological terms (a gene at +2 is 4× higher). However, fold change can be inflated for low-expression genes where small absolute differences produce large ratios. Best for identifying strongly upregulated or downregulated genes in absolute terms.
  • −log₁₀(padj) — transformed adjusted p-value: statistical significance of the difference. Sensitive to sample size — with thousands of cells, even tiny differences reach significance. Useful for confirming a gene is reliably different (not noise), but the top-ranked genes may have trivially small effect sizes. Best used as a filter (is this real?) rather than a ranking (is this important?).

In both modes, clicking a gene loads its expression on the UMAP. Selecting a gene auto-scrolls the ranked list to that gene's position.

Red-flagged genes: Genes shown in red in the ranked list are ones that would have been excluded by traditional cutoff-based methods (scanpy's highly_variable_genes). The specific reason is shown in the hover tooltip — either low mean expression (< 0.0125), high mean expression (above the adaptive ceiling), or low detection rate (< 0.5% of cells expressing). These genes are not excluded from FLEEK's ranking — the locally standardized z-score handles them naturally. The red flag is a transparency feature: it lets you evaluate whether high-ranking red-flagged genes represent genuine biological signal that traditional methods would have missed, or artifacts that the model hasn't fully corrected for. If many red genes appear near the top, treat the rankings with extra scrutiny.

Gene index — on load, a CSC (compressed sparse column) index is built in the background for fast per-gene lookups. A spinner in the Data panel shows progress. Before the index completes, gene lookups use a slower row-scan fallback but still work. The index is cached to disk (.csc_cache.npz) — subsequent loads skip the build entirely.

View controls: Point Size, Opacity, and Unselected Brightness sliders in the View panel let you fine-tune the visualization. Point Size is normalized 1–100 (default 7%). Opacity controls global transparency of cells. Unselected Brightness fades non-selected cells toward the background — this value is maintained separately per color mode: Gene Expression mode defaults to ~2/3 brightness so expressing cells remain visible through unselected ones, while Cell Type/Cluster mode defaults to ~1/3 brightness for stronger contrast. Both values are set automatically when the first selection is made, and switching color modes swaps to the appropriate brightness. Manual slider adjustment is saved per mode.

Keyboard nudges for size and opacity: press + / − to step Point Size up or down by 5 (range 1–100), and ⌥ Opt++ / ⌥ Opt+− to step Opacity by 0.05 (range 0.05–1). Holding the key continues to advance at a controlled cadence — slower than the OS native key-repeat rate so the slider lands where you want.

Partial visibility indicators: Cell type dots, lineage tree dots, and slice pills all show proportional fill when some of their cells are hidden by other filters. A dot that's half-filled means roughly half of that cluster's cells are visible. Hover over any dot or pill for exact counts. A hidden-cell counter appears in the bottom-left HUD.

Selection highlight rings: scale proportionally with cell size — they get thinner when zoomed out and thicker when zoomed in, always sitting on the cell's visual circumference.

Expression rendering: In Gene Expression mode, expressing cells can optionally have their gradient edge removed for a flat disc appearance when halos are active, reducing visual noise. Non-expressing cells retain the soft gradient. This switches automatically when Expression Glow is toggled.

Gene Info panel: click the floating gene pill at the top of the main viewer to open a draggable Gene Info modal. The top block is a thin bundled info card — full name, one-line summary, aliases, chromosomal location, gene type, Entrez ID — fetched on demand from mygene.info and cached locally under ~/.fleek_cache/gene_info_<species>.json so each unique symbol is only fetched once per organism. The second block is an organism-aware links grid that opens authoritative resources in your default browser:

  • All species: NCBI Gene, UniProt, Ensembl, PubMed, AlphaFold.
  • Human: GeneCards, Human Protein Atlas, Open Targets, OMIM, GTEx.
  • Mouse: MGI / Mouse Genome Informatics.
  • Zebrafish: ZFIN. Fruitfly: FlyBase. Rat: RGD. Nematode: WormBase.

External resources are never iframed — most block cross-frame embedding via X-Frame-Options, and keeping them as plain outbound links means the user always sees the authoritative site in its native UI. Offline note: external links need internet. If mygene.info is unreachable (HPC node with no egress), the info block degrades to a "couldn't reach gene info service" message but the links grid is still rendered from the symbol alone, so they'll work later when you're back online. The modal is closable with Esc, the close button, or a click outside; its header is draggable if you want to reposition it while keeping the main viewer visible.

▼ Plots

The Plots panel lets you render summary visualizations of gene expression. The Type dropdown picks the plot:

Raincloud plot: per-cluster expression distribution for a single gene. Each cluster column has three layered components:

  • Density curve (half-violin, right side) — the distribution shape of cells that actually express the gene, histogram-smoothed with a 5-tap gaussian filter.
  • Raindrops (dots, left side) — every expressing cell is drawn as a tiny semi-transparent dot. Horizontal placement is beeswarm-style: dots at similar y values are spread evenly across the full width of the dot column, with alternate-row offsets so adjacent y-bands don't line up into vertical stripes. Each dot's y is exact — no vertical shift. Zeros are subsampled and piled at y=0 so the zero mass is visible without dominating the render.
  • IQR bar + median tick (center, gray) — inter-quartile range and median computed across all cells in the cluster (including zeros). For zero-inflated genes the box collapses near y=0, which is the truthful signal that most cells don't express the gene.

Violin plot: the same per-cluster density mirrored to both sides of the centerline (the classic Seaborn / scanpy violin look), with the IQR bar + median tick running through the violin's center. No raindrops — the violin alone conveys both the shape and detection rate (when Scale width by % expressing is on). Use it when you want a denser, more familiar figure that emphasizes distribution shape over per-cell visibility, and reach for the raincloud when you want to see every cell. The underlying computation is identical, so switching plot types is instant — no re-fetch.

Dot plot: a gene × cluster grid summarising a panel of marker genes at a glance. Rows are genes (in input order), columns are clusters (visible cell types only), each dot encodes two things at once:

  • Dot size ∝ √(% of cells in that cluster expressing the gene). Sqrt scaling matches the raincloud's width-by-% — keeps a 5% cluster legible without making 1% clusters disappear.
  • Dot color = mean expression in that cluster, mapped through the active gradient (the same one the gene-coloring picker uses). A bright dot = high mean, a dim dot = low mean.

Two encodings together let you read the standard "small-but-bright = rare-but-strong; big-but-dim = common-but-weak; big-and-bright = ubiquitous-and-strong" pattern that Seurat's DotPlot and scanpy's sc.pl.dotplot standardised.

Gene input: type a gene name and a live autocomplete dropdown shows matching symbols. Click a suggestion or press Enter to add it as a chip. ↑ / ↓ walk the dropdown; comma or semicolon while typing also commits the current token. Press Backspace in an empty input to remove the last chip; click any chip's × to remove it directly. Pasting a comma-, newline-, tab-, or space-separated list (e.g. a column copied from Excel or a marker panel from a paper) validates each token against the dataset and turns the matches into chips automatically — tokens not found in the dataset are reported in the status line beneath the panel. Up to 64 genes per render; beyond that the plot becomes hard to read.

Color scale per gene (default on): each row's color scale is normalised to its own min/max — answers "which clusters express this gene most?" Off: a single shared scale across all genes — answers "which gene is highest expressing overall?" but compresses low-expressing genes to a flat dim row. Use per-gene for marker panels, global for absolute-expression comparison.

Mean over all cells (default on, scanpy / Seurat convention): mean expression for the dot color is computed over every cell in the cluster, so a cluster where 5% of cells express at level 1.0 has mean ≈ 0.05 — detection rate is encoded in both size and color. Off: mean is computed only over expressing cells, so size encodes detection rate and color encodes "how strongly the cells that do express express."

Min cells per cluster (default 5): hide cluster columns with fewer cells than this after the visibility / slice filters have been applied. Dot-plot statistics on a cluster of 3 cells are nearly meaningless; the threshold removes that noise from the figure. Set to 0 to show every visible cluster regardless of size.

Gradient: the dot-color gradient picker appears in two synchronised places — the Plots panel (so you can pick before rendering) and the popup header of the open plot (so you can swap gradients while looking at the result). Both show a list of gradient previews (Viridis, Plasma, Inferno, Cividis, Reds, Blues, …) with a strip swatch next to each name; click one to apply. Auto follows the global gene-coloring picker (View panel) and flips with theme; any other entry overrides only the dot plot. Changing from the popup re-renders the open plot from cached data immediately — no re-fetch. The popup-header Color toggle is hidden for dot plots because it doesn't apply (the dot plot's encoding is gene × cluster, not the violin/raincloud per-cluster palette).

Download: the popup's download button (top-right) saves the dot plot as a single SVG (faceted plots combine into one file). Same icon and behaviour as the raincloud / violin export.

What's plotted reflects the active view. Visible cell types (from the Cell Types panel) determine which clusters appear; the active slice filter further subsets cells. Change the main view to change what's plotted — there's no duplicate filtering UI in the Plots panel.

Facet splits the plot into side-by-side panels by any obs column that (a) has between 2 and 20 non-empty values in the dataset, and (b) still has ≥ 2 of those active under the current Slice. Columns pinned to a single value are hidden from the dropdown (they'd yield a pointless one-panel facet). The upper cap of 20 is pragmatic: past that, individual raincloud panels get too narrow to read, and the limit also guards against accidentally faceting by a high-cardinality column (sample ids, barcodes) that would spawn hundreds of empty panels. If you need more panels and are comfortable with the space, that number is a one-character bump in the source. Combine slicing with facets to build nested views — e.g. slice to timepoint = D8 and facet by organ for a "cell types within a single timepoint but across organs" figure. When the Slice panel's Show Empty toggle is off, facet values with zero cells are dropped as whole panels and don't count toward the cardinality threshold. Empty cell types (clusters with 0 cells) are always dropped — the raincloud never draws an empty cluster column.

Scale width by % expressing: when on (default), the density curve's horizontal width is scaled by √(% expressing), so a cluster where 5% of cells express the gene has a narrower curve than one where 80% do. This mirrors the standard scRNA-seq dot-plot convention (dot radius ∝ √fraction so dot area ∝ fraction) and makes the plot encode both the detection rate (width) and the distribution shape of the expressing cells (shape inside the width) simultaneously. A 2 px floor keeps ~1% clusters still visible. Turn off for shape-only comparison.

Why sqrt specifically: Pure linear scaling (width ∝ % expressing) would compress 5–10% clusters into an unreadable hair, losing the distribution shape entirely. Sqrt keeps the horizontal room proportionally larger so low-detection distributions stay legible. The trade-off: slight perceptual overweighting of small-detection clusters vs. the "mass-correct" linear encoding.

What counts as "expressing": a cell's normalized expression value ≥ 0.01 on the [0, 1] scale returned by the server. That scale comes from value / p98(positive values) with clipping — so 0.01 is "1% of the 98th-percentile expressing cell." In practice this means any cell with detectable source expression counts as expressing (matching Seurat's DotPlot and ScanPy's sc.pl.dotplot defaults). The threshold was picked to sit safely above normalization noise without requiring an absolute minimum count. Note: a single transcript can be ambient RNA or sequencing noise in scRNA-seq, not "real" biological expression — so interpret low % expr values with that caveat in mind.

Density scope: the density curve uses expressing cells only (value ≥ 0.01) by default. Including zeros would make every cluster's density peak at y=0 and dominate the shape regardless of cluster, so clusters would look visually identical. The raindrops still show zeros so you can see the non-expressing cells; the box plot and median also use all cells by default so they reflect the true distribution including the zero mass.

Include zeros in density / Include zeros in IQR: the two toggles in the Plots panel let you flip those defaults per plot. Turn Include zeros in density on if you want the curve to literally show the full per-cell distribution including the zero mass (note: the zero spike will dominate every cluster, so this is useful mainly for single-cluster reference figures, not for cross-cluster comparison). Turn Include zeros in IQR off if you want the median and IQR bar to describe the spread of cells that actually express — independent of detection rate — instead of the truthful "most cells don't express, so median = 0" picture. Re-render after toggling.

Boundary handling: normalized expression is bounded on [0, 1], so a naive smoother biases the density inward at the edges — a cluster piling up at 100% would show its smoothed peak visibly below where the raindrops pile up, because the gaussian kernel gets truncated against the boundary. FLEEK uses two standard bounded-KDE corrections: (1) reflection smoothing — kernel taps that fall past the edge are mirrored back into the support, preserving total mass and keeping the mode flush against the boundary; and (2) edge-flush rendering — the density path is extended from each bin's center to the plot's top and bottom edges with the edge bin's width, closing the half-bin visual gap between the curve and the raindrop pile. Peaks at 0 or 1 now align with the raindrops instead of hovering half a bin away.

Labels: each cluster column is labeled with its full name, total cell count, and detection rate — e.g., T cell (4,521 · 42% expr). Labels rotate 90° (straight down) so horizontal footprint is zero and adjacent cluster labels never overlap regardless of length. The bottom margin scales dynamically with the longest visible label — short names → compact plots, long names → taller plots. Hover any label for the native tooltip as a redundant affordance.

Y-axis units picker (raincloud / violin only). Live in two synchronised places — the sidebar Plots panel and the open plot's popup header (left of Color). Five choices:

  • Normalized [0, 1] (default) — per-gene 98th-percentile rescale, clipped to [0, 1]. Good for shape comparison within a single gene; cross-gene magnitudes are hidden because every gene's max becomes 1.0.
  • log1p(CP10K) — canonical scanpy / Seurat unit. Server forces log1p(counts / library × 10,000) from the raw-counts layer. Typical max ~5; log1p(10) ≈ 2.40, log1p(100) ≈ 4.62. Cross-gene magnitudes are directly comparable, which is what most published figures use.
  • log1p(CPM) — same idea as CP10K but per million; values run ~4.6 higher. Typical max ~10.
  • adata.X (as-is) — returns whatever's literally in the loaded display matrix without any post-processing. Useful when the file already encodes a specific transform you want to preserve untouched.
  • Raw counts — integer counts straight from the raw-counts layer, no normalization.

Choices that need a raw-counts layer (CP10K, CPM, raw counts) are greyed out when the dataset doesn't have one. Hover the Y-axis label inside the open plot for a tooltip explaining what's currently being shown. Switching units re-renders the open plot immediately. The dot plot and main-viewer coloring stay on the [0, 1] scale because they need a global scale; this picker only affects raincloud and violin.

Column spacing slider in the popup header (raincloud / violin only — dot plot is intentionally locked to a fixed pitch). Range 50%–250%, default 100%. The slider adjusts the GAP between columns; the violin / density / raindrop shape stays at its baseline width regardless. This is on purpose: scaling the subplot width with the slider would make the same distribution look fatter at higher spacing settings, breaking cross-plot comparison. Now a wider plot is just spread further apart, never fatter — distributions are comparable across spacing settings. The slider redraws live (every input event) so dragging it is interactive. Drag-to-reorder hit zones follow the new spacing automatically. Persists across reloads.

Show zeros as raindrops toggle (raincloud only). On (default) keeps the existing behavior — a stride-sampled set of zero-expressing cells gets stacked at y=0 so the non-expressing fraction is visually represented in the raindrop strip. Off omits zeros entirely from the strip; only expressing cells get drawn as dots. Useful when most cells in a cluster don't express the gene and the zero pile visually dominates. The density curve and IQR bar are controlled by their own zero toggles independently.

Dot plot tooltips: hover any cluster column label, gene row label, the % expressing legend label, or the mean expr color-bar label for a one-line explanation of what that axis or encoding shows. The cluster name still appears alongside the per-column tooltip, and the gene name with the per-row tooltip, so you can read both at once.

Reorder columns by drag: grab any cell-type column on a raincloud, violin, or dot plot and drop it left or right to move it. While you drag, the picked-up column dims in place, a small floating chip with the cluster's color and name follows the cursor, and a cyan dashed line marks the drop position. The new order propagates across every facet of the plot and is remembered for the rest of the session (persisted to sessionStorage, so a refresh keeps your ordering). Small accidental clicks (under the drag threshold) don't reorder anything.

Stopping a render: click the Render button again (it flips red and reads "Stop") to abort an in-flight gene fetch. Useful when you queued a render on a dataset that needs a slow first-time fetch.

Popup controls: the plot opens in a floating window centered on screen. The header has a download button (saves the current plot as a standalone .svg file, named by plot type + gene + facet) and a close button. Drag the header to reposition the window — useful for comparing a plot against the main canvas or another panel without closing it. Press Esc or click anywhere outside the window to close. Theme changes (dark ↔ light) re-skin the open plot in place with no re-render cost — the computed distribution is cached and only the SVG styling is rebuilt.

▼ Pathways

The Pathways panel tests whether your gene list overlaps with curated gene sets (KEGG-style canonical pathways, GO terms, MSigDB Hallmark, etc.) more than expected by chance. The first version implements ORA (over-representation analysis) — a hypergeometric test, BH-corrected. GSEA (preranked) will be added in a future release with the same UI; both methods will share this panel.

What ORA actually does: for each pathway in the chosen database, it asks "given the dataset's full gene list as background, how surprising would it be to draw your input list and have k of the input genes happen to land in this pathway?" The answer is a one-sided hypergeometric p-value. Across all tested pathways, p-values are corrected with the Benjamini-Hochberg step-up procedure to control false discovery. Significant hits at padj < 0.05 mean "this pathway is enriched in your list more than chance — the overlap is bigger than you'd get from random sampling."

Source picks where the input gene list comes from:

  • Current DEG result — uses the most recent DEG run (Selection Groups → DEG button). Top N genes by |log₂FC|, with a Direction filter (up / down / either) so you can ask separately about up- and down-regulated programs.
  • Cluster vs rest — uses the cached one-vs-rest cluster-genes table for the chosen cluster (the same table powering the per-cluster gene-variability list). Top N genes by Cohen's d (effect size), so very-small-fold-but-very-clean markers don't get lost. Pick a cluster in the Gene panel's variability list once to populate the cache for that cluster.
  • Paste gene list — drop in any list, one gene per line (space, comma, semicolon, or tab also work as separators). Useful when you have an external hit list from another paper / tool.

Top N defaults to 100 — a conventional ORA size. Smaller (50) for cleaner enrichments, larger (250–500) when you want to catch subtler pathway signal at the cost of more multiple-testing burden. There's no perfect choice; a careful workflow is to run at 100 and 250 and see which pathways are robust to the cutoff. The fundamental cutoff-sensitivity of ORA is exactly why GSEA exists, and why we'll add it later.

Database picks the gene-set library:

  • MSigDB Hallmark (50 sets) — broad, well-curated, the best single-database choice for a first pass. CC-BY-4.0.
  • Reactome (~2500 sets) — canonical biological pathways. CC-BY-4.0.
  • GO Biological Process / Molecular Function / Cellular Component — exhaustive but redundant. Useful for deep dives. Loaded from FLEEK's existing GO database (no extra files).

Drop additional .gmt files into data/gmt/<organism>/ to expose them; the server scans that directory on dataset load and the panel picks them up automatically.

Background: by default the dataset's full gene list is the universe. This is reasonable but slightly biased — if your dataset only profiles a curated panel, the background is small and tests are conservative. Custom backgrounds are not exposed in this version; if you need them, you can edit the request body.

Reading the results table: rows are sorted by adjusted p-value (most significant first). Each row shows the pathway name, a bar for log₂ fold-enrichment (how many times more overlap than expected — bar normalized to the strongest hit in the result), the overlap count (k / K = overlap / pathway size after intersection with background), and adjusted p in the tooltip. Click a row to expand the overlap genes as chips. Click any chip to load that gene's expression on the main viewer.

Selecting cells from a pathway: the "+ Select cells expressing these" button (in the expanded row) adds every cell expressing any overlap gene above the standard threshold (≥ 0.01 on the normalized scale) into the active selection group. The button is a smart toggle — once every expressing cell is already in the group, its label flips to "− Deselect these cells" and a click strips that population back out (cells you added by other means stay). Option/Alt+click the button to put only these cells into the active group (clears the group first). The same actions are also available directly from the pathway name in the row header: Shift+click the pathway name = toggle, Shift+Option+click the pathway name = select only — useful for sweeping through a long list of significant pathways without expanding each one.

Caveats: redundant gene sets (KEGG/Reactome share many pathways with GO BP) will produce correlated significant hits — don't double-count. Tiny overlaps (1–2 genes) are rarely meaningful even when the test reports "significant" — eyeball the overlap count column. Background choice subtly biases results; in particular, scRNA datasets often filter out lowly-expressed genes upstream, which means truly tissue-specific pathways may have artificially low K and miss enrichment.

▼ DEG analysis

The DEG button (enabled when 2+ groups have cells) opens the analysis panel at the bottom. Select two groups to compare, choose a test, and click Run. The Run button becomes a red Stop button during computation — click to cancel (the client aborts its fetch and the server stops at the next checkpoint).

Tests available:

TestDescription
WilcoxonRank-sum test. Non-parametric, robust for scRNA-seq. Recommended default.
t-testWelch's t-test. Faster, assumes roughly normal expression in each group.

Gene filtering: Before testing, genes expressed in fewer than 3 cells across both groups are removed. This typically reduces ~60k genes to ~10–20k, giving a 3–6× speedup without affecting results for meaningful genes.

Input matrix & normalization: FLEEK routes DEG to the best-available matrix via the hierarchy described in Data matrices & routing. Preference: raw counts (normalize+log1p applied on the fly) → log-normalized layer as-is → denoised counts (normalize+log1p, with caveat). The Data panel shows which matrix was picked and any caveat. When running on denoised data, p-values and Cohen's d will be anti-conservative (within-group variance is artificially small); gene rankings are still meaningful but statistical magnitudes are unreliable.

Volcano plot: log₂ fold-change on x-axis. Y-axis toggles between −log₁₀(padj) and |Cohen's d|. Dotted lines mark significance thresholds: |log₂FC| ≥ 0.5 (vertical lines) and padj < 0.05 or |d| ≥ 0.8 (horizontal line). Genes passing both thresholds are colored by group direction. A horizontal dotted line labeled "top N%" shows the visibility cutoff — genes below this line are hidden by the "Show top N%" slider. When viewing all 100% of genes, the visibility cutoff line is hidden. Default 50% shows the most significant genes, keeping the plot responsive.

Volcano navigation: Scroll to zoom both axes simultaneously. Drag the plot to pan. Drag the x-axis area (bottom) left/right to zoom just the x-axis (fold-change); drag the y-axis area (left) up/down to zoom just the y-axis (significance). Double-click to reset all axes to default view.

Interacting with results: Hover over a volcano point or table row to see gene stats and highlight the corresponding element in the other view. Click a gene (in either the volcano or the table) to load its expression on the UMAP — this lets you immediately see where a differentially expressed gene is active in the cell landscape. The gene search box in the DEG table filters results in real time. Table columns (gene, log₂FC, Cohen's d, padj) are sortable — click any header to sort.

Resizable panel: Drag the divider between the volcano plot and gene table to adjust their relative sizes. The layout is preserved during the session.

Download: Click the download button to export the full DEG table as a TSV file (tab-separated values), suitable for spreadsheets or downstream analysis tools.

Cohen's d is computed directly on the sparse matrix using E[X²]−E[X]² with Bessel correction, avoiding densification.

Caching & history: Both single-cell and pseudo-bulk runs land in the History dropdown at the top of the DEG panel. The cache key records the test type (wilcoxon / t-test / pb-deseq2 / pb-ttest), the replicate column for pseudo-bulk runs, and the selection-group combination — so switching between backends or replicate columns stores results as distinct entries instead of overwriting. Click any entry to restore its volcano + gene table instantly. A "stale" badge marks entries where the selection groups have changed since that analysis was run; the results are still viewable but may no longer match the current cell assignments. History is persisted in session state, so it survives page reloads.

Pseudoreplication caveat: Cells from the same donor/sample are not independent observations. P-values from single-cell comparisons are anti-conservative — they reflect intra-sample variability, not inter-donor. For publication-level results, use the pseudo-bulk mode described below.

Pseudo-bulk mode: Switch between Single-cell and Pseudo-bulk using the toggle at the top of the DEG panel. Pseudo-bulk aggregates raw counts per biological replicate within each selection group, then runs a bulk-level statistical test. This addresses the pseudoreplication problem.

Workflow:

  1. Select cells into Group A and Group B (e.g., two cell types, two conditions, or any two subsets).
  2. Open DEG panel and switch to Pseudo-bulk mode.
  3. The Group A / Group B dropdowns remain visible — select which two groups to compare.
  4. A single Replicate column dropdown appears. Select the obs column that defines biological replicates (e.g., donor, batch, sample_id). Columns with 2–50 non-empty unique values are listed — when the Slice panel's Show Empty toggle is off, empty values aren't counted toward the threshold, so a mostly-empty column drops out of the dropdown.
  5. Pick a Method: DESeq2 (default) or Welch's t-test (CPM). Leave on DESeq2 unless you specifically want to compare backends or DESeq2 is misbehaving on your dataset.
  6. Click Run. For each group, cells are split by replicate, raw counts are summed per replicate, and the resulting pseudo-bulk count matrix is tested.

Statistical tests: pyDESeq2 is used by default — it models count dispersion across replicates using a negative-binomial GLM. The pre-built desktop apps ship with pyDESeq2 bundled, so it's the default backend out of the box (single-threaded inside the bundle — loky/joblib worker processes don't survive in a frozen executable). Picking the Welch's t-test (CPM) option from the Method dropdown bypasses DESeq2 entirely; the server also falls back to this automatically if DESeq2 fails at runtime (the server console prints the actual failure reason).

Requirements: A raw counts matrix must be detected in the dataset (checked automatically on load — see the Data panel). Each group needs cells in at least 2 replicates. More replicates improve statistical power. Genes with fewer than 10 total counts across all samples are filtered out.

Counts source visibility & smoothed-count guard: The Pseudo-bulk controls show which matrix was detected as counts (e.g. adata.X, a layer, or adata.raw.X) along with median UMI/cell, max, and the computed median Fano factor (variance/mean across moderately-expressed genes). Real UMI data is overdispersed (median Fano typically > 1.5) because of biological cell-to-cell variation; model-generated integer counts (scVI denoised_counts, MAGIC, similar denoisers) follow a Poisson distribution with Fano ≈ 1. Pseudo-bulk is disabled when the detected matrix is unambiguously smoothed: Fano < 1.0 (under-dispersed; impossible for true raw counts), or Fano in [1.0, 1.5) with a layer name that hints at smoothing (denoised, smooth, magic, imput, scvi, saver, dca). The borderline zone (Fano 1.0–1.5 without a smoothing hint) is treated as raw — homogeneous cell populations and tightly-filtered datasets can produce real UMI counts in this range, and gating pseudo-bulk off for them was too conservative. If gated, an amber banner explains why: on Poisson-distributed counts the negative-binomial dispersion estimate collapses near zero, yielding anti-conservative p-values — everything looks significant. This guard prevents silently invalid DEG results when a denoised matrix is mistaken for raw counts.

Results: Displayed in the same volcano plot and gene table as single-cell DEG. The note above the plot shows the method used, group names, replicate counts, and number of genes tested. Cohen's d is not computed for pseudo-bulk (shown as 0).

Cancel mid-compute: During either a single-cell DEG or pseudo-bulk run, the Run button turns red and displays Stop. Click it (or refresh) to abort. The server checks for the abort flag at natural checkpoints (each replicate aggregation, before pyDESeq2 fit/summary, every 512 genes in the t-test fallback) so cancellation takes effect promptly without leaving the server spinning. The status line names the backend (pyDESeq2 vs t-test (CPM)) while running.

▼ Data management

RNA-FLEEK loads .h5ad files (AnnData format) in three ways: upload (click the upload icon or select a file), drag-and-drop (drag an .h5ad file onto the Data panel), or browse (click the folder icon to navigate the server's file system — no upload needed for local files).

File browser: Finder-style column navigation — click a directory to see its contents in a new column to the right.
