Swipe horizontally (trackpad or click-and-drag) to navigate up and down the hierarchy. The path bar at the top is editable — type or paste a path, press Tab for completions, Enter to navigate. Single-click a file to see load options, double-click to load immediately. Cache status dots show which files have cached embeddings, gene indices, or annotations.

Unloading: Click the power icon (⏻) in the Data panel header to unload the current dataset and free all server memory. This stops any background processing (CSC indexing, PaCMAP computation) and clears all caches.

Before loading, a popover lets you configure:

OptionDefaultDescription
QuickMap (UMAP)On (50k)Subsample-and-project UMAP. Fits on a stratified subset, projects the rest via PCA transform. Much faster for large datasets with near-identical quality. Adjustable 10k–200k cells.
QuickMap (PaCMAP)On (50k)Subsample-and-project PaCMAP. Fits on a subset, projects via k=5 inverse-distance weighted interpolation in PCA space. Smooth interpolation avoids discretization artifacts.
QuickDEGOn (50k)Subsample cells (stratified by cluster) for annotation DEG. Marker genes are virtually identical on 50k vs 600k+ cells, but 10–20× faster. Adjustable 10k–200k cells.
Disk-backedOffKeeps the expression matrix on disk. Slower gene lookups but allows datasets larger than available RAM.
Native 2D embeddingsOffCompute 2D embeddings independently instead of slicing 3D coordinates. Produces tighter 2D cluster separation at the cost of longer load times. If a native 2D cache already exists, it is used automatically regardless of this setting.

The Data panel is collapsible — click the header to expand/collapse. It auto-collapses after data loads to save space. The upload button is always accessible in the header row.

Uploads can be stopped mid-transfer with the Stop button. Starting a new upload automatically cancels any in-progress upload. When loading a new dataset, the old dataset's memory is fully freed.

Automatic clustering: If the dataset has no cell_type or leiden column, FLEEK automatically computes Leiden clustering using the igraph backend (building a neighbor graph and PCA first if needed). Results are cached for instant reload.

Organism detection: FLEEK infers the organism (Human or Mouse) from gene names. It first checks for species-specific marker genes (e.g. GAPDH/CD3D for human, Gapdh/Cd3d for mouse) — if ≥3 markers match, the species is determined. Otherwise, it falls back to a gene-name casing heuristic (UPPERCASE → human, Title case → mouse). The inferred species is shown in the Data panel info line as "(inferred)" with a tooltip explaining the reasoning. It is used to load the correct Gene Ontology database.

Empty clusters: Some datasets contain cell type categories with zero cells. This typically happens when a dataset has been subsetted (e.g. filtered to a single tissue) without cleaning the categorical column — the original categories persist even though no cells remain for some types. It also occurs in concatenated datasets where certain types exist only in specific batches. FLEEK shows these as dimmed entries with an "empty" label — they are non-interactive since there are no cells to visualize or select.

Dual caching: Quick and full versions of embeddings are cached separately — switching between QuickMap on/off doesn't overwrite the other version. Both coexist in the cache file, and loading picks whichever matches your current setting. DEG results similarly coexist in memory (quick and full are separate cache entries). This means you can freely switch between fast and precise modes without recomputing anything after the first run of each.

Cache fileContents
.fleek_cache.npzUMAP quick+full (2D+3D), PCA (50 components), PaCMAP quick+full (2D+3D), Leiden cluster assignments
.csc_cache.npzCSC gene index for fast per-gene lookups
.annot_markers.jsonMarker-based cell type annotations
.annot_llm.jsonClaude LLM cell type annotations
.annot_lineage.jsonClaude-generated developmental lineage tree

Badges in the Data panel show active settings (QuickMap, QuickPaCMAP, QuickDEG) and which caches are loaded. Cache badges appear as green pills (or colored for Markers/Claude/Lineage). Hover over any cache badge to reveal a × on the left — click it to delete that cache. A confirmation dialog appears before deletion. Deleting an annotation cache clears the in-memory annotations and re-enables the corresponding button. Cache badges appear immediately when new caches are created (e.g. after running Markers or Claude). Cache files are validated against cell count — if the h5ad changes, stale caches are automatically discarded.

Server startup: python fleek_server.py [file.h5ad] — or start without a file and use the file browser or upload. For Claude annotation, set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in your environment or in ~/.fleek.env. Use --host 0.0.0.0 to allow connections from other machines. SSH sessions are auto-detected (no browser auto-open).

Remote deployment: Run FLEEK on a remote server (e.g. HPC cluster) and access via SSH tunnel: ssh -L 9123:127.0.0.1:9123 user@server, then open http://localhost:9123 locally. The server processes data where the files live — only small payloads (coordinates, expression values) travel through the tunnel. Use pip install -r requirements.txt to install dependencies; pacmap<0.8 avoids GCC version issues on older HPC systems.

Exports: Click the save icon on a selection group to download that cell subset as an .h5ad file. Exported subsets include sliced embeddings (UMAP, PCA, PaCMAP), cluster color maps, and annotation caches — so when you load a saved subset, it appears instantly with colors and cell type predictions already in place, no recomputation or API calls needed. You can also save all cells matching the current slice filter from the Slice panel. Large exports are streamed in 4MB chunks.

Subset annotation remapping: When you load a subset that reuses its parent's annotation cache, FLEEK automatically remaps cached cluster IDs by matching cluster name — the subset's categorical codes may shift if unused categories got dropped, but predictions stay aligned to the right cell types. The server log prints "remapped for subset" when this kicks in.

Data source subsection: Inside the Data panel, the collapsible "Data source" drop-down lists every expression matrix FLEEK found in the h5ad (raw counts, denoised counts, log-normalized, scaled), along with quick stats (median UMI/cell, max value, median Fano factor) and which one was chosen for each analysis task. Amber caveats appear when a non-ideal fallback had to be used. See Data matrices & routing for full detail.

▼ Data matrices & routing

A single .h5ad can contain several expression matrices — adata.X, adata.raw.X, and any number of adata.layers[...]. These usually hold different stages of processing (raw UMI counts, denoised counts, log-normalized, scaled/z-scored, etc.). FLEEK inspects every matrix at load time, classifies it, and routes each analysis task to the most appropriate one. The "Data source" dropdown in the Data panel lists every matrix found and what it's being used for.

KindHow FLEEK detects itTypical content
raw countsInteger values, reasonable per-cell totals, median Fano ≥ 1.0 (or 1.0–1.5 with no smoothing hint in the matrix name)Direct UMI counts from CellRanger / STARsolo. What pseudo-bulk DEG actually needs.
denoised countsInteger values, but median Fano < 1.5 (Poisson-like)Output of scVI denoised_counts, MAGIC, SAVER, or similar. Looks like counts but biological overdispersion has been regressed out.
log-normalizedDecimal-valued, non-negative, max < ~100Output of sc.pp.normalize_total + sc.pp.log1p. Standard for visualization, PCA, Wilcoxon-DEG.
scaledDecimal-valued, has negative valuesOutput of sc.pp.scale (z-score per gene). Useful for PCA input but unsuitable for Fano / DE on its own.

Task routing. Each analysis path picks the best-available matrix from this preference chain:

TaskPreference chainIf fallback is used
Gene variability (Fano-based)raw counts → log-normalized → denoisedOn log-normalized: trend method still works, but Fano interpretation is approximate. On denoised: rankings dominated by residual numerical noise, results unreliable.
Single-cell DEGraw counts + normalize+log1p → log-normalized as-is → denoised + normalize+log1pOn denoised: p-values and Cohen's d are anti-conservative (within-group variance artificially low); rankings still meaningful.
Pseudo-bulk DEGraw counts onlyNo fallback — pseudo-bulk is disabled if no raw-counts matrix is found or if the detected counts look smoothed.
Gene expression displaylog-normalized → raw counts + normalize+log1p on-the-fly → denoised + normalize+log1pOn raw: normalized per-column for display so colormap gradients are comparable across cells. On denoised: same, but gradients may look artificially smooth.
Embedding computation (if none in obsm)log-normalized → raw counts + normalize+log1p → denoised + normalize+log1pPre-computed embeddings in obsm are always used as-is if present; the fallback only fires when FLEEK has to compute a fresh PCA/UMAP.

Why FLEEK does this automatically. scRNA-seq pipelines vary. Some labs store raw counts in adata.X and compute everything downstream on the fly. Others store log-normalized data in X and keep raw counts in adata.raw.X or a layer. Others run a denoiser and overwrite X with its output. Without routing, FLEEK would silently run Fano math on log-normalized data, or run Wilcoxon on smoothed counts and produce implausibly tiny p-values. Routing picks the right matrix for the right job and, when a fallback is used, shows a caveat in the Data panel so you can judge whether to trust the result.

Check the Data panel. The "Data source" drop-down shows every matrix found with its classification (colored pill) and quick stats (median UMI/cell, max value, median Fano). Below that, each task is listed with which matrix it routed to and any caveat that applies. Amber ⚠ markers flag non-ideal fallbacks.

Pseudo-bulk's Fano guard. When a detected counts matrix has median Fano < 1.5 (i.e., looks like model-generated Poisson data rather than real UMIs), pseudo-bulk DEG is automatically disabled. The reason: DESeq2 and pyDESeq2 estimate negative-binomial dispersion from the data, and on Poisson-distributed counts the dispersion collapses near zero, producing anti-conservative p-values where essentially everything looks significant. Better to refuse the test than run a fictitious one.

▼ Sessions

Auto-save: FLEEK auto-saves your session state every 2 seconds. This includes selection groups, camera position, active gene, coloring mode, slice filters, view splits (with their per-split camera and tether state), individually deleted cells, DEG history (single-cell and pseudo-bulk), and panel states. State persists across browser sessions — reloading the page restores exactly where you left off. The session dropdown always shows an Auto-saved entry even before the first auto-save has committed, so picking (Fresh session) and then resuming work just rejoins the auto-save stream.

Named sessions: Use the Save As button to save the current state as a named session, stored alongside the dataset caches. Named sessions let you maintain multiple analysis configurations for the same dataset.

Session dropdown: The Data panel includes a session dropdown to switch between saved sessions. Loading a session restores the full UI state including camera position, active gene, selections, and visibility.

Delete: Click the trash icon to remove a named session (with confirmation). The auto-save session cannot be deleted manually — it is overwritten on every state change.

Reset: Click the Reset button for a fresh start — clears all session state and returns to defaults.

DEG results are saved with sessions, so switching back to a named session restores your previous differential expression results without recomputation.

Undo/redo history is saved with sessions. Your full action history is preserved and restored when switching sessions or reloading.

▼ Undo & redo

FLEEK tracks your actions and lets you step backward and forward through your work. Press ⌘+[ to undo or ⌘+] to redo (Windows/Linux: Ctrl+[ / Ctrl+]). Optional undo/redo buttons can be shown in the top-right corner of the canvas — toggle their visibility in Settings.

What is tracked:

StateExamples
Selection groupsLasso select, clear cells, add/remove group
Camera positionRotate, pan, fly, zoom, reset view, fly-to
Active gene & color modeLoading a gene, switching Cell Type / Gene Expression
VisibilityShow/hide groups, show/hide cell types, solo/unsolo
Slice filtersToggle filter values, reset filters
Panel statesCollapse/expand sidebar panels

History is capped at 50 entries. Performing a new action while partway through the undo stack discards the redo future (standard undo behavior). The full undo/redo history is saved with sessions and restored when switching sessions or reloading the page.

▼ Settings

Click the gear icon in the top-right corner to open the Settings popup. Changes take effect immediately and are persisted in the browser.

SettingOptionsDescription
ThemeDark / LightSwitches the entire UI and canvas between dark and light themes. Light mode uses a Blues sequential colorscale for gene expression (low expression fades into the white background). Dark mode uses Viridis. Press ` (backtick) to toggle.
UI scale75%–150%Adjusts the size of all UI elements — sidebar, panels, text, buttons. Useful on high-DPI displays or when using FLEEK on a projector.
Hint barOn / OffThe hint bar at the bottom of the canvas shows contextual tips about modifier keys and available actions. Disable for a cleaner view once you know the shortcuts.
Shortcut flashOn / OffBrief overlay that flashes on screen when you press a keyboard shortcut, showing the action name and key combination. Helps learn shortcuts. Disable for a quieter interface.
Undo/Redo buttonsOn / OffShow or hide the undo/redo arrow buttons in the top-right corner of the canvas. The keyboard shortcuts ⌘+[ / ⌘+] (or ⌘+Z / ⌘+Shift+Z) always work regardless.
Auto-quit server on disconnectOn / Off (off by default)When on, FLEEK's background server auto-quits after a short grace period (default 20 seconds) once the last browser tab has closed. It unloads the dataset and exits the process, so there's no lingering local server eating RAM or holding a port. A page reload within the grace window cancels the quit. Off by default — turn on if you'd prefer closing the tab to also shut down the server. Timeout can be tuned in the Debug panel.
Claude API keyText input + Save / Test / ClearEnter your Anthropic API key and click Save — it's written to ~/.fleek.env (chmod 600) on the server. Test sends a minimal ping to api.anthropic.com and reports back the model name on success, or the verbatim Anthropic error body (401 / 403 / 429 / network) on failure — paste a key into the field and click Test to verify before saving, or leave the field empty and click Test to re-verify the already-saved key. Clear removes it from memory and the file. If the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var is set at server startup, it takes priority over the saved file.
Cache modeDataset / ServerDataset stores caches alongside the .h5ad file (portable — cache moves with the data). Server stores caches in ~/.fleek_cache/ (works with read-only or networked data). If the dataset directory is not writable, FLEEK falls back to server mode automatically.

Debug panel (click gear icon, then scroll down): Contains developer-oriented settings including Zoom target (Cursor/Center), FPS counter, Selection highlight mode (Ring/Fill), Unload timeout (seconds), raycast visualization, and background scheme. These are primarily for troubleshooting and advanced use.

Point-size auto-scaling: FLEEK adjusts the default point size based on cell count so sparse datasets look legible and dense ones stay tight. All embeddings normalize to the same spatial extent, so without this correction a 1 k-cell dataset would render as scattered pinpricks while a 1 M-cell dataset packs tightly. The point-size slider still works normally on top of this — the scaling only changes what "50 %" looks like per dataset.

▼ Quick reference

Modifier-key labels below match the OS you're running on. Mac: ⌘ (Cmd) and ⌥ (Option). Windows/Linux: Ctrl and Alt.

Navigation
Left-drag3D rotate / 2D pan
Ctrl/⌘+drag3D pan
Right-drag3D pan
⌥ Opt+drag3D fly through
Middle-drag3D fly through
ScrollZoom in/out
Double-click cellZoom-focus on cell (preserves distance for nearby clicks)
Hover over cellHighlight cell, show tooltip with cluster & expression info
Selection
Shift+dragLasso select cells into active group
Shift+⌥+dragLasso deselect
Shift+click (UMAP)Toggle single cell in active group
Shift+dblclick (UMAP)Toggle entire cell type in active group
Shift+click (Cell Types)Toggle cell type into / out of active group
⌥+Shift+click (Cell Types)Isolate cell type — clear active group, then select only this type
⌥ Opt+click (legend dot)Solo / unsolo cell type
Lineage View (dot = this node only, name = node + descendants)
Click nameToggle visibility of branch (node + descendants)
Click dotToggle visibility of this node only
⌥+nameSolo branch (node + descendants)
⌥+dotSolo this node only
Shift+clickSelect branch into group (dot = this only)
⌥+Shift+clickSelect only this node (not descendants)
Caret (▸/▾)Collapse/expand branch (⌥+click: recursive)
View splits
1–9Switch to Split N by name (creates if needed)
⌘+1–9Switch to Nth split in the panel list (no create)
SToggle split-screen (requires 2+ splits)
⧉ buttonToggle split-screen (requires 2+ splits)
Chain-link iconToggle tether per split — works even in single-view mode
Groups
Shift+1–9Activate group (creates if needed)
⌥+Shift+1–9Toggle group visibility
Shift+HHide/restore all group highlights
Shift+VSelect all visible cells into active group
DeleteArbitrarily hide individual selected cells in active group (off by default — enable in Settings: "Allow arbitrary cell hiding")
⌘+DeleteHide entire clusters containing selected cells
EscClear visible cells in active group / close dialogs
⌥ Opt+EscClear ALL cells in ALL groups
Lists (vim-style)
JMove down in gene/cell type list
KMove up in gene/cell type list
Visibility
HShow all cells (press again to restore previous hidden state)
RReset camera view
⌥+RReset camera + show all cells + show all groups
Display
CColor by cell type / cluster
GColor by gene expression (single gene)
LColor by coLocalization (two-gene merge)
IToggle Info overlay (cell counts / color mode / active group)
DToggle 2D/3D view
ECycle embedding (UMAP → PCA → PaCMAP)
`Toggle dark/light theme
TToggle camera tether on the active split
⌥+← / → / ↑ / ↓Move the active highlight to the adjacent split
\Toggle sidebar
⌥+,Open Settings
SToggle split-screen (side-by-side) — requires ≥2 view splits
Slice panel
Click pillToggle slice value
⌥+click pillSolo this value (or restore all if already solo)
⌘+⌥+click pillMake all cells of this value fully visible
General
⌘+ZUndo
⌘+Shift+ZRedo
⌘+[ / ]Undo / Redo (alternate)
/ or ⌘+KSearch palette — keyboard-driven jump to any panel, mode, toggle, or action. Type a few characters of what you want, ↑/↓ to navigate, Enter to land. Recently-used items appear first when the input is empty. The selected target is briefly spotlit in the UI so you can see exactly where you landed.
EnterConfirm the active dialog: Save a session prompt, Delete a confirm dialog, Export the points (Export modal), Save the API key (Settings), Load a chosen file. The primary action button is the green / accent one in the dialog.
EscClose any open dialog (Help, Plot popup, Export, Confirm, Prompt, Gene info, Feedback) — falls back to clearing the visible cell selection when no dialog is open.
Drag file onto Data panelUpload .h5ad dataset
▼ Methods & statistics

UMAP is computed via scanpy using default parameters (n_neighbors=15, min_dist=0.3). Both 2D and 3D embeddings are computed and cached to disk. QuickMap fits on a stratified subsample (preserving cluster proportions) and projects remaining cells via batch PCA and UMAP transform. Quick and full versions are cached separately (umap_2d_quick / umap_2d_full) — switching between modes never overwrites the other. Legacy caches are accepted as fallback.

PCA — the first 2 or 3 principal components are extracted from the full PCA matrix (50 components by default). PCA is typically computed as part of the UMAP pipeline (scanpy requires it for the neighbor graph) so it adds no extra cost. The full 50-component matrix is cached and restored to adata.obsm["X_pca"] so downstream methods (PaCMAP, neighbor graph) can use it without recomputation.

PaCMAP (Pairwise Controlled Manifold Approximation) operates on the first 30 PCA components. It uses a three-stage optimization: (1) early iterations weight far pairs heavily to establish global layout, (2) mid-pairs prevent false neighbors (cells that are close in the embedding but distant in the original space), (3) late iterations refine local structure. Computed in a subprocess to avoid numba/threading conflicts with the server.

QuickMap (PaCMAP) uses subsample-and-project for datasets >50k cells. PaCMAP is fit on a stratified subsample, then remaining cells are projected via k=5 inverse-distance weighted interpolation: for each unfit cell, the 5 nearest neighbors in PCA space are found (cKDTree), and the cell is placed at the weighted average of their PaCMAP coordinates (weights ∝ 1/distance). This produces smooth interpolation — cells between clusters land between them proportionally, avoiding the discretization artifacts of simple nearest-neighbor copy. Quick and full versions are cached separately.

Leiden clustering is computed automatically when a dataset lacks cell_type or leiden annotations. Uses the igraph backend with resolution=1.0 and 2 iterations. Requires a neighbor graph — if none exists, FLEEK builds one from PCA (or computes PCA from scratch if needed). Results are cached in the unified .fleek_cache.npz file.

Differential expression uses scanpy's rank_genes_groups. P-values are adjusted for multiple testing using Benjamini-Hochberg. Genes expressed in fewer than 3 cells are filtered before testing. Clusters with fewer than 5 cells are excluded from annotation DEG. If Wilcoxon fails (e.g. edge cases with sparse data), the server falls back to t-test automatically.

DEG subsampling (QuickDEG): For large datasets, the annotation DEG is run on a stratified subsample (default 50k cells) rather than the full dataset. Each cluster contributes cells proportionally, with a minimum of 5 per cluster. The top marker genes are virtually identical whether tested on 50k or 600k+ cells — the additional cells only push p-values lower without changing rankings. This is a separate mechanism from the user-facing DEG panel (which always tests the specific selection groups on their actual cells).

Shared DEG: Both marker-based and Claude annotation share a single DEG computation. The first to run computes the one-vs-rest rankings, caches them in memory (keyed by cell count, cluster count, test method, and subsample size), and the second method gets the results instantly. Quick DEG (subsampled) and full DEG (all cells) are cached separately — switching between QuickDEG on/off doesn't invalidate the other. A threading lock ensures concurrent requests don't conflict.

Cohen's d (effect size) is computed as the difference in means divided by pooled standard deviation, with Bessel correction. Computed directly on the sparse matrix via E[X²]−E[X]² to avoid materializing dense arrays.

Marker-based annotation uses the shared one-vs-rest DEG results (top 200 genes per cluster after housekeeping/MT/ribosomal filtering), then scores each cluster against every cell type signature using: (1) a rank-weighted overlap score (markers at the top of the cluster's DEG list count more), and (2) Fisher's exact test for enrichment, with the universe restricted to expressed genes in this dataset. Predictions are ranked primarily by p-value. The built-in database contains ~55 cell types; the full CellMarker2 database adds ~800+. When PanglaoDB is also installed (scripts/download_panglao.py), both DBs are ensembled and predictions agreed on by both are flagged with a "both" source tag.

LLM annotation uses the same shared DEG results, formatted as a structured prompt to Claude (Anthropic). The top 25 marker genes per cluster are sent in batches of 50 clusters. Claude returns cell type, confidence level, and reasoning. Each batch uses up to 8,192 output tokens. The model evaluates gene combinations holistically rather than matching individual genes against a list.

Gene variability scoring — FLEEK ranks all genes by how variable they are relative to their expression level. When a dataset already contains a variability metric (checked in order: dispersions_norm, dispersions, variances_norm, variances in adata.var), that metric is used directly. When no pre-computed metric exists, FLEEK computes its own using the locally standardized trend residual method described below.

Which matrix feeds the variability calculation: routed per Data matrices & routing. Ideal is raw counts (Fano interpretation is statistically clean); log-normalized is an acceptable fallback (trend/residual method still works, though Fano as a Poisson-vs-NB diagnostic no longer applies); denoised counts are a last resort (Fano trend collapses to ≈1 because the denoiser fits a Poisson emission, so the rankings end up dominated by numerical noise). The Data panel shows which matrix was used for variability and whether a caveat applies.

The problem (two parts):

Part 1 — mean-variance dependence: In scRNA-seq data, variance scales with mean expression. Highly expressed housekeeping genes (ACTB, GAPDH, Malat1, ribosomal proteins) dominate raw variance rankings simply because they are highly expressed, not because they are biologically informative. A good variability score must correct for this dependence.

Part 2 — heteroscedastic scatter: Even after correcting for the mean-variance trend (e.g. via Fano factor or simple polynomial residual), the scatter around the corrected trend is not uniform across expression levels. At very low expression, residuals are noisy — Poisson counting statistics dominate, and a gene expressed in 2 out of 50,000 cells can produce a large positive residual from pure chance. At moderate expression, the same residual magnitude would be genuinely remarkable. A raw residual of +1.5 means something very different depending on expression level. Scores must account for this expression-dependent noise.

Existing approaches and their limitations:

MethodLimitation
Raw variance (σ²)Biased toward highly expressed genes — ACTB will always rank near the top regardless of its biological informativeness
Coefficient of variation (σ/μ)Biased toward barely-detected genes — a gene in 2 out of 50,000 cells gets an extreme CV from a single stochastic count
Binned z-score (scanpy dispersions_norm)Discretizes the smooth mean-variance relationship into 20 bins, then z-scores within each bin. Requires arbitrary expression cutoffs (min_mean, max_mean, min_disp) — genes outside these thresholds are silently excluded, potentially discarding real biological signal
Simple polynomial residualCorrects the mean-variance trend (solving Part 1), but does not account for heteroscedastic scatter (Part 2) — barely-detected genes with large Poisson residuals still rank at the top

FLEEK's approach: locally standardized trend residual. A two-stage method that corrects for both the mean-variance trend and the expression-dependent noise level, without arbitrary cutoffs.

Stage 1 — Trend correction:

StepOperation
1Compute per-gene mean (μ) and variance (σ²) across a random subsample of up to 50,000 cells
2Compute the Fano factor for each gene: F = σ²/μ (for genes with μ > 0)
3Transform both axes to log₁₀ space: log₁₀(μ) vs log₁₀(F)
4Fit a degree-3 polynomial to the trend: log₁₀(F̂) = a₃·log₁₀(μ)³ + a₂·log₁₀(μ)² + a₁·log₁₀(μ) + a₀
5Compute raw residual for each gene: r = log₁₀(F) − log₁₀(F̂)

Stage 2 — Local standardization:

StepOperation
6Fit a second degree-3 polynomial to |r| vs log₁₀(μ). This estimates the local scatter envelope σ̂(μ) — how much residual spread is expected at each expression level
7Compute locally standardized z-score: z = r / max(σ̂, 0.1). The floor of 0.1 prevents division by near-zero at expression levels where genes happen to sit tightly on the trend

Interpretation: The z-score is comparable across the full expression range. A z-score of +3 means the gene is 3 local standard deviations above the expected Fano factor for its expression level — regardless of whether it's a moderately expressed gene or a highly expressed one. True cell-type markers (expressed strongly in one cell type, absent in others) produce high z-scores because their excess variance is large relative to the local noise floor. Housekeeping genes score near zero because their variance falls on the trend. Critically, barely-detected genes also score near zero: their raw residuals may be large, but so is the local scatter at that expression level — the two cancel out.

Why two stages instead of one: A single polynomial fit corrects the trend (Part 1) but treats all residuals as equally meaningful. The second fit corrects the noise envelope (Part 2). Together, they produce a score where +2 means the same thing at log₁₀(μ) = −3 as it does at log₁₀(μ) = 0. This is analogous to how a z-score in statistics standardizes by both subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation — doing only the first step leaves you with residuals that are still not comparable across conditions.

Why degree 3: The mean-Fano relationship in log-log space typically shows curvature at both extremes — a cubic polynomial captures this without overfitting to local noise. This applies to both the trend fit and the scatter fit. Higher degrees risk fitting individual gene outliers; lower degrees miss the curvature and produce systematic biases at the tails.

Why no cutoffs: Every gene with detectable expression receives a z-score. There are no arbitrary thresholds on minimum mean, maximum mean, or minimum fraction of expressing cells. The local standardization naturally handles the noise at expression extremes — no gene needs to be excluded because its z-score already reflects the noise level at its expression level. This eliminates a class of silent errors where biologically relevant genes at expression extremes are dropped without the researcher's knowledge.

Cutoff transparency flags: Although FLEEK does not apply cutoffs, it computes which genes would have been excluded by traditional methods (min_mean < 0.0125, max_mean above the 95th percentile, or detection in < 0.5% of cells). These genes are flagged in red in the ranked list, with the specific reason shown on hover. This provides a built-in sanity check: if many red-flagged genes rank near the top, the local standardization may not be fully correcting for noise at that expression range, and the researcher should evaluate those genes individually. The flags are purely informational — they do not affect the scores.

FlagMeaning
Not expressedMean expression = 0 across the subsample
Low meanMean < 0.0125 (scanpy default min_mean)
High meanMean above the adaptive ceiling (max of 3.0 or 95th percentile)
Low detection rateExpressed in < 0.5% of cells

Why log-log space: The mean-variance relationship spans several orders of magnitude. Fitting in linear space would be dominated by a handful of very highly expressed genes. The log transform compresses the dynamic range, giving equal weight to genes across the full expression spectrum.

Subsample: For datasets exceeding 50,000 cells, mean and variance are computed on a random subsample (seed=42 for reproducibility). Gene-level statistics converge rapidly — the variance estimate from 50k cells is nearly identical to the full dataset for all but the rarest genes, at a fraction of the compute cost.

Comparison with pre-annotated metrics: When a dataset already has dispersions_norm (from scanpy's highly_variable_genes), FLEEK uses that directly. The locally standardized trend residual and dispersions_norm usually agree on the top ~500 genes. Differences appear (a) in the long tail, where binning artifacts produce different rankings than the smooth polynomial, and (b) at expression extremes, where cutoffs in dispersions_norm exclude genes entirely while the trend residual gives them near-zero scores. Both are valid; the trend residual avoids discretization artifacts, cutoff dependency, and heteroscedastic bias at the cost of being less established in the literature.

Expression normalization for visualization uses 98th percentile of expressing cells as the ceiling, mapping to [0, 1]. This prevents a handful of extreme outliers from washing out the color range.

Point rendering uses Three.js WebGL with circle-textured point sprites. Point size is controlled by a normalized 1–100 slider, converted to world units relative to the cloud bounding sphere (max = 1.8% of cloud radius). In 3D, sizeAttenuation provides perspective-correct depth. In 2D, world units are converted to pixels via orthographic frustum math with a 0.5× scale factor, ensuring 2D points are proportionally smaller than 3D (appropriate for the flat view where depth fill isn't needed). Point selection uses distanceToRay rather than camera distance — in dense 3D perspective scenes, this picks the point actually closest to where you're pointing. Ring highlights use a second point cloud layer with a ring texture, rendered with depth-test disabled so they're always visible.

Reset view fits the cloud precisely to the viewport in both modes. 3D computes the exact perspective distance: radius / sin(min(verticalHalfFOV, horizontalHalfFOV)) with 5% margin. 2D computes the ortho zoom level using the constraining axis. Both account for aspect ratio.

Performance: The CSC gene index takes ~5 minutes on 1.6M cells but is cached to disk for instant reload. PaCMAP runs in a subprocess to isolate numba JIT from the server's threading model. All heavy computation runs in background threads — the visualization is interactive immediately. Annotation DEG is subsampled to 50k cells by default (QuickDEG), reducing annotation time from ~10 minutes to ~30 seconds on large datasets. User-facing DEG pre-filters to expressed genes for 3–6× speedup. Volcano plot rendering uses batched Path2D operations (3 draw calls instead of thousands). All embeddings are dual-cached (quick and full versions coexist) alongside the h5ad file in a unified .fleek_cache.npz. Canvas coordinates are refreshed every frame via syncCanvasSize() to prevent selection offset drift after layout changes.

▼ Changelog

User-facing changes by version. Most-recent first. Internal refactors / performance work is omitted unless it affects how the app feels.

v0.5.57

  • Standardized text inputs. Every search box, name field, API-key input, and modal text input now shares the same sunken look — slightly recessed surface with an inset shadow that reads as a "pit you can type into," distinct from buttons that sit on the surface. Focus pulses an accent ring around the field. Works in both themes.
  • Volcano plot performance. Hovering over dots, panning, or dragging the axes used to stall the rest of the interface — the volcano was repainting all 30k gene points many times per second on the main thread. Renders are now rAF-throttled (one repaint per frame max), and the percentile-cutoff sort is cached so it only recomputes when the DEG result or Y-axis metric changes. Per-frame cost dropped from ~12 ms to ~3 ms on a typical DEG.
  • Faceted plot alignment. Dot plots used to drop a cluster from a facet entirely if it had fewer than the min-cells threshold in that panel — facets ended up with different cluster axes and were impossible to compare side-by-side. The cluster is now kept as an "absent" placeholder column (italic, dimmed label "(N · absent)") so the cluster axis stays in lock-step across every facet. Raincloud/violin already preserved alignment but the placeholder column now reads "(0 · absent)" instead of the misleading "(0 · 0% expr)".
  • Coloc-aware recent-gene badges. In the Colocalized tab, recent-gene badges now show channel highlights — the gene loaded into the R slot lights up red/magenta, the G slot green, and a gene loaded into both slots gets the composited coloc color. Recents are shared between Single and Colocalized tabs, so a gene picked in either lands in the badge cluster for both.
  • Plot scrollbars are always visible. The plot popup canvas and the DEG gene table now show persistent 12-px scrollbars when content overflows, instead of macOS hiding them until you scroll.
  • Faster gene picking. Mashing through genes via J/K used to pile up dozens of in-flight server requests and the latest pick could wait 20+ seconds for the backlog to drain. Each new pick now cancels prior in-flight fetches via AbortController, and auxiliary fetches (raw counts, per-unit variants) are debounced — they only fire when you pause on a gene.
  • Plot popup chrome cleanup. Background gradient slider, single-gene gradient, and Coloc gradient now share identical 13-px height + 5-px end-roundness. Coloc-tab spacing tightened so the slot row sits right above the gradient.
  • Settings → Claude model dropdown opens cleanly now (was rendering off-screen due to a containing-block issue).
  • Server bumps its file-descriptor limit at startup so casual gene-list mashing can't exhaust the macOS default; threading layer is auto-selected to a threadsafe option (TBB on Linux/Windows, single-threaded fallback on macOS).

v0.5.56

  • Linked hover on the gradient bar + picker button. Hovering either the single-gene gradient strip or its Viridis-style picker beneath now brightens BOTH (same for the Coloc strip + Magenta + Green picker). Each gradient is wrapped in a .grad-group; group-level :hover applies the brightness filter to the bar and the highlight colors to the picker simultaneously.
  • Thin gray border restored on all three gradients — single-gene strip, Coloc strip, and the Background-color slider all carry border:1px solid var(--border) again for visual definition against the sidebar surface.
  • Background-color slider is now per-theme. Nudging the slider in dark mode no longer overrides the light-mode bg (and vice versa). Each theme has its own user-set override; switching themes restores that theme's last choice (or the theme default if none was set). The default link only resets the active theme. Older sessions with the legacy single-value override are migrated to apply to both themes once on load.
  • Tighter Split-box right padding (2px 3px 2px 5px) brings the download + tether icons closer to the right edge for visual balance.

v0.5.55

  • Expression-mode toggles indented under Cell color with a faint left border, so it reads as "settings that only apply when Cell color = Gene expression."
  • Plot-canvas scrollbars are persistent (overflow:scroll instead of auto): when content overflows, the scrollbars stay visible — no relying on macOS overlay-mode auto-show.
  • Three gradient bars unified — single-gene gradient, Colocalized gradient, and the Background-color slider now share height: 8px and border-radius: 2px with no outer border. Squarer ends, thinner profile, visually consistent.
  • Capitalization sweep: all UI labels, panel headers, dropdown options, button text, search-palette entries, flash-action toasts, and help-section titles now use sentence case (acronyms still uppercase). "Selection groups", "Cell types", "Gene expression", "Render plot", "Show empty", etc. CLAUDE.md gets a new convention note.
  • Single dark rim color replaces the dual adaptive rim. The cluster-color luminance clamp added in v0.5.52 keeps cell colors out of the unreadable extremes, so a single dark rim reads consistently against every dot — the previous adaptive light/dark picker is removed. Debug menu now has one "Rim color" picker (default near-black).
  • "default" link in the VIEW panel sits right next to "Background color" so its scope is unambiguous.
  • Coloc legend bar lost its old gray border (still part of v0.5.52's clean-up — now formally documented in CLAUDE.md alongside the new height + roundness convention).

v0.5.54

  • Background color is now a black→white grayscale slider in the VIEW panel, with Cell color sitting underneath. Drag the thumb left for a darker canvas, right for lighter; a small default link snaps back to the current theme's tinted bg. The slider thumb auto-tracks the rendered bg's Rec. 601 luminance, so reopening the panel after a theme switch shows the thumb where you'd expect.
  • Coloc cell tooltip is two-line: cluster name on the top line, both gene readouts (with their channel-color dots) on a second line beneath it. Easier to scan than the previous single-line "cluster · R-gene: x · G-gene: y" format. The tooltip's whitespace mode also flips to normal so long gene names wrap instead of getting cut off.

v0.5.53

  • Coloc legend now shows whenever the Coloc tab is active, regardless of whether a gene has been picked or whether you arrived from cluster mode. Previously two gates conspired to keep it hidden in some flows: an ordering bug in _setGeneMode (visibility was checked before mode was promoted to "gene"), plus a selGene gate on the Single legend that meant flipping tabs while neither single's gene nor coloc's mode were set left both legends invisible. Both gates dropped; each tab's legend now follows only its own tab state.
  • VIEW panel: Cell color and Background color on one row — 50/50 grid. The bg picker's reset button is now a small default link directly under the picker (replacing the inline button on the row above), so the two controls visually match.

v0.5.52

  • Coloc legend extremes are no longer black/white. The bar was a 5-stop ramp (bg → R → coloc → G → bg) that faded to the canvas background at both ends to "represent non-expressing cells" — but the canvas already shows that, so the fades read as accidental black borders against the dark sidebar. Changed to a clean 3-stop ramp R-only → coloc → G-only: the bar now shows just the cells-that-got-colored palette.
  • Cluster colors are luminance-clamped. Some saved palettes in h5ad files contain very-dark or very-light entries that effectively disappear against same-tone theme backgrounds (and the adaptive rim alone isn't enough at the extremes). Every color now passes through _clampLuminance inside the shared cc() resolver: Rec. 601 luminance < 0.18 gets blended toward white, > 0.85 gets scaled toward black. Hue is preserved as much as possible. Applies automatically to every callsite (canvas, legend, cell-type list dots, plot panels, exports) — no setting to flip.
  • Help / About has new entries explaining the coloc legend bar interpretation and the cluster-color clamp.

v0.5.51

  • Display units row moved below the gradient in the Single tab — gradient bar + Low / picker / High row now sit above, with the Display-units row directly under that.
  • Coloc tab renamed to Colocalized; slot-row borders are tighter (padding 5/8 → 2/5, radius 4 → 3); slot placeholder reads "pick gene…" so the dots fit even at narrow sidebar widths.
  • Coloc gets its own Display units picker, restricted to the fair-mixing set: Normalized [0, 1], log1p(CP10K), log1p(CPM). Raw counts and adata.X (as-is) are intentionally excluded — channels with incomparable absolute ranges would mix unfairly (one gene's counts visually drowning out the other). Within the offered three the on-screen coloring is essentially identical (each channel is per-channel p98-normalized to [0, 1] before mixing); the picker mostly chooses what value reads in the cell tooltip. Picking shares the same per-split slot the Single tab uses.
  • Coloring-aware in coloc: coloc cells are now colored from the chosen unit's per-channel-normalized array (instead of just the legacy normalized cache). Falls back to the legacy cache while a unit's data is still streaming in, then re-paints automatically when the fetch completes.
  • Coloc tooltip now reports each channel's value in the chosen unit (percentage for Normalized, two-decimal float for log1p variants). Gene names are escaped through _esc for safety.
  • Coloc dot size convention documented in the help panel: dot area is proportional to the SUM of the two channels' [0, 1] normalized intensities (sqrt of sum drives the radius), so co-expressing cells visibly pop without overrunning the canvas. The convention holds across all units because the size encoding always reads the per-channel-normalized arrays — switching units doesn't shift dot magnitudes.
  • Switching to the Colocalized tab while an unsafe unit (Raw counts / adata.X) is active in the Single tab automatically coerces the active unit to Normalized [0, 1] so the channel-mixing scale stays fair. Switching back to Single doesn't reset; the change is one-way at the boundary.

v0.5.50

  • GENE panel renamed to GENE EXPRESSION in the sidebar header.
  • Single tab restructured: gene search box and recent-gene badges are now directly under the Single | Colocalization toggle (no more scrolling past the legend to swap genes). The "Expression: <gene>" label is gone — the gene name is already on the canvas indicator and in the recents list. The gradient picker dropdown is now centered between the Low and High labels, anchored to the gradient bar's visual center via a 3-column grid.
  • Colocalization tab restructured: the redundant "Colocalization" header above the gradient is gone. Search sits directly under the toggle, then the slot row — R-channel name with its dot at the LEFT, G-channel name with its dot at the RIGHT — sits ABOVE the gradient strip with the dots positioned over their respective ends of the gradient. The scheme picker (Magenta + Green / Red + Green) now sits centered below the gradient.

v0.5.49

  • Background color picker in the VIEW panel: pick any color for the main viewer's background, with a small reset link to revert to the current theme's default. Persists across reloads.
  • The VIEW panel's Color label is now Cell color to disambiguate from the new background-color control.
  • Display units trigger uses a lighter / theme-appropriate fill so it stands out against the sidebar (matches the look of native input boxes like Top N): elevated dark fill in dark mode, white in light mode.
  • Dropdowns auto-flip up when they'd otherwise be clipped at the bottom of the viewport. The Y-axis units menu, gene Display units, claude-model picker, and plot-popup type picker all share the same positioning helper now — open one near the bottom of the sidebar and it pops upward instead of cutting options off.
  • Debug menu → "Reset to defaults" button (top of the cell-rendering sections) reverts every slider, color picker, toggle, dropdown and the preset choice in Ghost dots / Expressing dots / Adaptive rim / Universal back to their out-of-the-box defaults. Glow & pulse parameters are NOT touched (they have their own feel).
  • Plot-canvas overflow indicators: soft fade gradients on the right and bottom edges of the open-plot canvas appear only when content actually scrolls past them, plus the scrollbars are now always-visible in the canvas. No more wondering whether there's more plot off-screen.
  • Adaptive rim — stronger boost for very dark cells. When a cell's luminance falls below ~0.30, the rim alpha ramps up to ≥ 0.85 (saturating at lum = 0.04) so even near-black cluster colors get a clearly visible outline against dark backgrounds. Mid-tone and light cells keep the original rim intensity.
  • Plot-popup type-dropdown chevron tweak: nudged 1 px down for a slightly better optical alignment with the label baseline.
  • Note in CLAUDE.md: cluster / cell-type palettes should keep entries in a moderate luminance band (0.18 ≤ L ≤ 0.82); extremes disappear against same-tone theme backgrounds even with the rim boost.

v0.5.48

  • Adaptive rim color for cells in the main viewer. The cell-type palette has both very dark and very light entries, and the legacy "darken-the-cell" rim disappears against same-tone backgrounds (dark cells lose their rim on dark theme; light cells lose theirs on light theme). The shader now picks the rim color per cell from its luminance: cells below 0.5 luminance get a light rim, cells above get a dark rim, so every cluster has a visible outline regardless of palette extremes. Default ON.
  • Debug menu (Expressing dots section): new Adaptive rim color toggle plus Rim · light and Rim · dark color pickers. The pickers default to white / black; tune them per theme if you want a more subtle outline. Toggling Adaptive off restores the legacy darken-the-cell rim.
  • Plot-popup type-dropdown chevron alignment — the ▾ next to "Raincloud plot" / "Violin plot" sat too low (visual mass of the Unicode arrow is at the bottom of its em-box). Bumped up by 2 px so it lines up optically with the label text.

v0.5.47

  • Per-unit gradient mouseover explanations — hover the gradient bar in the GENE panel (or the Low / High labels under it) to see what "low" and "high" mean for the active Display unit. Each unit gets its own text: Normalized talks about p98 clipping, log1p(CP10K) gives typical p98 ranges, adata.X (as-is) and Raw counts explain the linear-max scale and outlier behavior.
  • "adata.X (as-is)" coloring is now visibly different from "Normalized [0, 1]". Previously both ended up p98-clipped to the same [0, 1] range — adata.X is typically already log1p(CP10K), and the "normalized" path is just p98 of that, so the two coloring arrays were identical. Now as-is and Raw counts use linear max-normalization (no percentile clip), so the heavy-tail distribution is exposed: a few high-expressing cells dominate the gradient and the bulk reads dimmer — distinct from the broad distribution you see under Normalized or log1p(CP10K).

v0.5.46

  • Display units now drive cell coloring — switching the GENE-panel Display units picker (Normalized [0,1] / log1p(CP10K) / log1p(CPM) / adata.X / Raw counts) re-colors the main viewer using that unit's distribution (clipped to its 98th-percentile of expressing cells). Same for split halves: each split colors with its own chosen unit. The gradient itself stays in [0,1] so the legend reads the same; only the SHAPE of which cells get which color changes. Falls back to the legacy normalized array while a unit's data is still in flight, then re-paints automatically when the fetch completes.
  • Multi-unit cell tooltip resilience — each unit slot now tracks loading vs. unavailable distinctly. loading… while the fetch is in flight, — when the server reports the unit isn't available for this dataset (e.g., a unit needs raw counts that this h5ad lacks), and the value once it's fetched. No more permanent … when a fetch silently fails. Switching splits also triggers a prefetch for the active half's gene so the tooltip is ready by the time you mouse over a cell.
  • Plot-popup type dropdown — chevron moved to the LEFT of the plot name and made larger / clearer (now reads as part of the control, not a tiny decoration). Dot plot was removed from the popup-title list — switching to dot plot from a single-gene rendered plot would orphan the gene-list input. The sidebar plot-type dropdown still offers all three.
  • Spacing-resize stability — facets no longer shrink vertically while the slider is being dragged. Plot panels are now flex-shrink:0, so when their total width exceeds the popup, the canvas scrolls horizontally instead of squashing each panel. Same applies to single very-wide facets: they stay at their natural size and the popup gets a horizontal scrollbar.
  • Gene indicator hover — the colored hover background on the top-center gene-name pill now fills the entire indicator interior (including the indicator's outer padding) instead of leaving a thin rim around the corners.
  • Settings → Claude model dropdown bug-fix carryover: the cost-estimate suffix is back, but only inside the open list — the closed trigger still shows just the model name.

v0.5.45

  • Gene Display units picker in the GENE panel (above the gradient). Choose between Normalized [0, 1], log1p(CP10K), log1p(CPM), adata.X (as-is), and Raw counts. Cell coloring stays on the [0, 1] scale (gradient needs a global range), but the cell tooltip and HUD info chip read from the chosen unit. Per-split: each split snapshot stores its own units choice, so left and right halves can show different units side-by-side for direct comparison.
  • Multi-unit cell tooltip: hovering a cell now shows all available expression measures stacked one per line, with the currently-displayed unit bolded and arrow-marked. Hover any line for a per-unit explanation (matching the plot-axis tooltip wording). The library-size footer remains for normalization context.
  • Plot-type dropdown in the popup title: click the plot name in the open plot's header to switch between Raincloud / Violin / Dot plot without going back to the sidebar. Each option has a one-line description in the dropdown.
  • Spacing slider jumpiness fully fixed: the popup window's width and height are now pinned at drag start so the popup doesn't recenter as panels grow. Combined with the existing nowrap pin, the panels now resize smoothly in place.
  • Wide-plot height stability: as a side-effect of the popup-window pin, height changes during resize are eliminated. Resizing produces horizontal scroll in the canvas instead of vertical re-layout.
  • Density compression prevented for raincloud and violin: the slider's minimum is now plot-type-aware (60% for raincloud, 85% for violin) so the data-encoding density curve never has to compress to fit. In raincloud at tight spacing, only the raindrop strip's jitter compresses (raindrops are visual encoding, not data); in violin the slider physically can't go below the safe floor.
  • Claude model dropdown rebuilt as a custom dropdown so the per-run cost estimate appears next to each model in the open list while the closed trigger shows just the model name. No more chevron overlap.

v0.5.44

  • Settings → Claude model dropdown: the chevron no longer overlaps the option text. The estimated cost suffix has been dropped from the labels — the dropdown now just shows the model name.
  • Spacing slider no longer reshuffles facets while dragging. During the drag the facet wrapper switches to single-row layout so panels resize in place; the normal multi-row wrap is restored on release.
  • Raincloud and violin subplots can no longer overlap at low spacing settings. Subplot half-width is automatically clamped so the half-violin (raincloud) or each violin lobe (violin) never crosses into the neighbor's column. Above 100% spacing the clamp is non-binding and subplots stay at their baseline width per Option A.

v0.5.43

  • Spacing slider is now gap-only for raincloud / violin: it changes the distance between columns but the violin / density / raindrop shape stays at its baseline width. Distributions are now comparable across spacing settings — a wider plot is just spread further apart, never fatter. Cross-plot comparison is honest by default.
  • Realtime spacing redraw: the slider now redraws the open plot on every input event, not just on release. Dragging it is interactive instead of guess-and-check.
  • Dot plot is decoupled from the spacing slider entirely. The slider is hidden when a dot plot is open; dot column pitch (and dot radius) stay fixed so cross-plot dot comparisons are stable regardless of slider state.
  • Dot plot mouseover explanations: hover any cluster column label, gene row label, the % expressing legend, or the mean expr color bar for a tooltip explaining what that axis / encoding shows.
  • Show zeros as raindrops toggle in the Plots panel (default ON, current behavior). Turn off to omit the zero-expression pile from the raindrop strip — useful when the zero stack at y=0 dominates the visual and you want a cleaner shape view.
  • "Include zeros in IQR" default flipped to OFF for raincloud and violin. The IQR bar / median tick now reports the spread of cells that actually express the gene by default, instead of collapsing to zero on mostly-non-expressing clusters. Toggle it back on if you want the prior behavior.

v0.5.42

  • Y-axis units picker in the Plots panel and the popup header (left of Color). Choices: Normalized [0, 1] (legacy default), log1p(CP10K) (canonical scanpy unit; typical max ~5), log1p(CPM), adata.X (as-is), and Raw counts. Items needing a raw-counts layer are greyed out on datasets that don't have one. The sidebar toggle from v0.5.41 is replaced by the same dropdown — both views stay in sync.
  • Y-axis label is now units-aware with a hover tooltip explaining what the axis is showing (e.g. "log1p(counts / library × 10,000). log1p(0)=0, log1p(10)≈2.40, log1p(100)≈4.62"). Hover the axis label to read it.
  • Column-spacing slider in the popup header (50%–250%, default 100%). Makes columns wider or narrower without changing the data — useful when columns crowd each other. Persists across reloads. The drag-to-reorder hit zones follow the new spacing.

v0.5.41

  • Y-axis: log expression toggle in the Plots panel. When on, raincloud and violin plots show real log-normalized expression magnitudes (whatever's in adata.X) and the Y-axis auto-fits the highest value across visible cells. Off (default) keeps the legacy [0, 1] per-gene rescale. The toggle solves two related complaints: high-expressing genes piling up against the top of the axis, and every gene's distribution looking the same shape because per-gene normalization rescales each one's max to 1.
  • Y-axis label switches to "log expression" in log mode. Tick labels show the actual range. The dot plot and main-viewer expression coloring continue to use the [0, 1] scale (they need a global scale to work).

v0.5.40

  • Drag-to-reorder columns in plots: Grab any cell-type column on a raincloud, violin, or dot plot and drop it left or right. The picked-up column dims in place, a floating chip with the cluster's color and name follows the cursor, and a cyan dashed line shows where it'll land. The new order propagates across every facet of the plot and is remembered for next time (per session, persisted via sessionStorage). Clicks under the drag-threshold are ignored, so a stray click won't reorder anything.

v0.5.39

  • HUD popups: The bottom-left Info overlay's Hidden and Embedding rows are now clickable. Clicking Hidden N opens an "Unhide:" drop-up menu listing every hidden cell type (with its colored dot), every hidden slice value (grouped by obs column, e.g. organ: Liver), every hidden selection group, and arbitrarily-deleted cells; first item is "all" to restore everything in that split. Clicking Embedding opens a switcher for that split's embedding (UMAP / PCA / PaCMAP). Outside click or Esc closes.
  • Per-split state isolation: Activating split mode (or expanding the count) now auto-clones the active view into a fresh snapshot for each extra quadrant, so cell-type and slice toggles in one split no longer leak into the others. Arbitrary deletes (Backspace) remain global because they operate on global selection groups.
  • Shortcuts: Theme toggle moved from T to ` (backtick). T now toggles the active split's camera tether. ⌥ Opt + arrow keys move the active highlight to the adjacent split.
  • Single-view label is a dropdown again with the same look as the split-mode view selector — pick a different split from the list to switch active. Click-to-rename was removed; rename is still available from the Split Views sidebar panel.
  • Canvas overlay margins unified at 8px (HUD, split labels, FPS box, history buttons, settings button) so every overlay has the same offset from the edge of its viewport.
  • Hover-brighten now works in every split (was only working in the top-left). Click-to-activate-split is more reliable (recomputes the hovered quad fresh on mousedown). H-key show/restore toggle works correctly in split mode (previously stuck after one press).

v0.5.38

  • Per-split embedding: Tethered halves share embedding (since they share a camera, they must share coordinate space) — switching the embedding from any tethered half changes the whole tethered group together. Untethered halves manage their own embedding independently. Re-tethering snaps a half's embedding to the master's.

v0.5.37

  • Pseudo-bulk DEG fix: The matrix classifier was rejecting valid raw-count matrices on huge datasets, leaving pseudo-bulk silently disabled. Classifier now uses a column-subsample that scales with gene count, a robust mean-of-data estimator for median library size, and a relaxed Fano gate (Fano < 1.0 is the only unambiguous "denoised" signal; the gray zone uses layer-name hints).
  • UMAP recompute: The Data panel's Recompute subsection can now rerun UMAP with custom parameters (QuickMap toggle, fit-cells subsample size, n_neighbors, min_dist) without restarting the server.
  • Library size in tooltip: Cell mouseover tooltips show Olfm4: 38.4% (3 raw / lib 4,210) — the percentage is the normalized display value, raw is the unnormalized count, lib is the cell's total raw counts. Makes the "fewer counts but higher %" pattern interpretable (it's just per-cell library-size normalization).
  • Repo cleanup: The dual fleek_server.py / rna_fleek/server.py and fleek.html / rna_fleek/fleek.html mirror pairs collapsed into a single source of truth. Top-level files are now a 3-line shim and a symlink respectively.

v0.5.36

  • Saved sessions are now tagged with the loaded h5ad path; per-dataset state (recent genes, selections, slice filter, deleted cells, active gene) is gated on a path match so a Mouse-dataset session no longer restores Title-case genes onto a Human dataset.

v0.5.35

  • Marker scoring v2: Annotation now uses rank-weighted overlap (top-N markers contribute more) with a hypergeometric universe, plus a housekeeping-gene mask (RPL/RPS/MT-/HSP/HIST + ACTB/GAPDH/B2M/etc.) to prevent dominant-but-uninformative genes from inflating scores.
  • PanglaoDB ensemble: When PanglaoDB markers are available, FLEEK averages CellMarker2 and PanglaoDB scores per cluster so cell-type calls aren't dominated by either DB's idiosyncrasies.
  • Lineage tree polish — tighter indents, aligned caret/dot/text, file-name + AI-name on separate lines (lavender for AI), Claude reasoning in tooltip.

v0.5.30 — v0.5.34

  • Dot-rendering pass — opaque dots, faux-3D rim, glow controls, occlusion-aware picker, expression-driven size with sum-of-channels scaling for coloc.
  • Splits + Selection Groups panels are scrollable + drag-resize.
  • Matrix-classify cache (~3 s saved per load), fast subset export, pan/rotate gain tweaks, ⌥ Opt + , for Settings.
  • Load-time speedup: 100 s → ~12 s on the canonical large-dataset benchmark.

For commit-level history, see github.com/tubuliferous/rna-fleek.

RNA-FLEEK · Built with Three.js, scanpy, and anndata

Format

Send feedback

Type

Your FLEEK version, browser, theme, current dataset name + dimensions, and active gene are attached automatically. Cell data is not sent.
